library.description: Turn a single staged listing photo into a cinematic, ad-ready video where the room empties, a branded box falls and explodes, and every detail is restored exactly as before.

library.title: Video Rough-Cut Editor Prompt
library.body:
Video Rough-Cut Editor Prompt

What This Is

A paste-ready prompt that turns Descript’s Underlord into your rough cut editor. Drop it in before you start editing, and the AI will scan your raw talking head footage — find the best takes, cut the bad ones, remove filler words, strip dead air, and even follow your verbal directions if you gave any during recording — as in, you said something like: “Use that last take.” It’s the first 80-90% of your edit, done in seconds.

This prompt is built for a specific recording style: scripted talking head videos where you repeat lines to get the best delivery. Whether you’re reading off a teleprompter, working from bullet points, or freestyling with retakes — if your raw footage has multiple attempts at the same lines, this is for you.

Video builds authority faster than any other content format. When someone watches you break down a market trend, walk through a listing strategy, or explain what buyers and sellers need to know right now — you become the expert they trust. That’s true whether it’s a 60-second Reel, a YouTube Short, or a 10-minute deep dive. The agents who post consistently are the ones who stay top-of-mind.

Here’s the problem: most agents know they should be posting more video. They have the ideas. They can record just fine. But the editing kills them. Not the creative part — the tedious part. Scrubbing through 15 minutes of raw footage to find the three good takes. Cutting the six bad ones. Trimming the dead air. Removing the “ums.” That rough cut phase is where videos go to die on a hard drive.

This prompt hands that entire job to Descript’s built-in AI editor: Underlord. You still do the creative polish — transitions, animations, music. But the grunt work of assembling the rough cut from raw footage? That’s handled.

If you’ve used Gling.ai (covered in the AI Video Editing Shortcuts lecture), you already know the concept — AI that finds and removes bad takes. This prompt does something similar, but inside Descript where you’re already editing, and it can follow your verbal directions mid-recording, which Gling can’t.

How to Use It

Record your video. Repeat lines as many times as you need until you’re happy with the delivery. Don’t worry about mistakes — the AI will sort it out.

Go to descript.com and log in (or open the Descript desktop app). A free account works for this.

Create a new project. In the left sidebar, click Projects, then New Project → Video Project.

Upload your raw footage. Drop your video file into the project. Descript will automatically transcribe it — this is what Underlord reads to make its editing decisions.

After the transcription is autogenerated, open Underlord. Look for the button in the lower-right corner of the screen. It opens a chatbot. You’ll see a model selector — it defaults to Auto, but you can choose a specific model. We recommend Claude Opus 4.6 for the most accurate results with this prompt. 

Copy the prompt below. Before you paste it, read the two recording style options inside the prompt and delete the one that doesn’t match how you recorded.

		Prompt

				Copy

You are a talking head video editor. Your job is to create a clean rough cut from this raw footage. The speaker recorded a scripted video and repeated lines multiple times to get the best delivery. Your job is to keep only the best take of each line and remove everything else.

RECORDING STYLE:
>>> IMPORTANT: Delete one of the two options below before pasting this prompt. Keep the one that matches how the video was recorded. <<<

OPTION A — LINE BY LINE: The speaker read one line at a time off a teleprompter, repeated it until they were happy, then moved to the next line. Duplicate takes of the same idea will be clustered together in the timeline.

OPTION B — FULL SCRIPT PASSES: The speaker read the entire script from top to bottom multiple times. Duplicate takes of the same idea will be spread across the timeline — the first attempt of each line will appear early, and later attempts will appear later in the footage.

DIRECTOR CUES — HIGHEST PRIORITY:
The speaker sometimes talks to the editor mid-recording. These are commands, not content. Look for phrases like: "let me do that again," "one more time," "scratch that," "use that one," "go with that take," "that was good," "that's the one," "take two," "from the top," "all right, that one's done," or any moment where the speaker is clearly addressing the editor rather than the audience. When you find a director cue:
- If it's a REDO signal ("let me do that again"), cut the bad take AND the cue itself. Keep the next attempt.
- If it's a KEEP signal ("use that one," "that was good"), keep the take they're referring to. Cut the others.
- If it's a CUT signal ("editor cut this," "don't use that"), remove what they're pointing to.
- The audience should never hear any director cue in the final edit.

DUPLICATE TAKES — WHEN NO DIRECTOR CUE EXISTS:
When the speaker says the same idea multiple times and gives no verbal instruction about which to keep:
- Keep the take with the fewest filler words
- Keep the take that completes the full thought (incomplete takes lose)
- Keep the take with the cleanest, most concise phrasing
- If it's a coin flip, keep the LAST take

REPEATED LINES WITHIN A SINGLE TAKE:
Sometimes the speaker repeats a line within the same continuous take — they say "not because it's hard" twice in a row without stopping. When this happens, keep ONLY the first clean instance of the line and cut where the repetition begins. Do NOT remove the entire take — surgically cut the repeated portion.

FILLER WORDS:
Remove all "um," "uh," "like" (when used as filler, not comparison), "you know," "I mean," "so yeah," and false starts where a sentence begins and is immediately abandoned.

DEAD AIR:
Remove all silence gaps longer than 1 second between lines.

NOT PART OF THE SCRIPT:
Any moment where the speaker breaks character — laughing at themselves, sighing, reacting to a mistake, talking under their breath, commenting on their own performance, or addressing someone off-camera — is NOT content. Cut these moments along with the bad takes they accompany. If it sounds like the speaker stepped out of "presenter mode," it's not part of the video.

CRITICAL RULES:- Never remove a line that is only said once. If the speaker makes a point only one time, it stays even if the delivery is imperfect.
- Every distinct idea in the video must be represented in the final cut. Do not drop entire topics.
- When cutting a repeated line within a take, make the cut mid-block — do not delete the entire block.
- Close all gaps so the kept sections play back to back smoothly.

			Show more

Paste the prompt into Underlord and send. It will scan your transcript and start making cuts across the timeline. Give it a minute to work.

Review and refine in the chat. This is the most important step. The first pass gets you about 85–90% of the way. After Underlord finishes, stay in the chat and ask: “Did you remove any lines that were only said once? Walk me through what you kept and what you cut.” If it dropped something it shouldn’t have, just tell it — “You removed the line about CapCut, put that back” or “That take at 0:45 was the good one, keep that instead.” You’re in a conversation. Work through it until the rough cut feels right.

Make your polish pass. Once the rough cut is clean, add transitions, captions, punch-ins, music, and color. That’s the creative work — and it goes fast when the rough cut is already done. (Btw, Underlord can help with some of these tasks too!)

If you’re new to Descript, watch AI Video Editing Shortcuts first — it walks through the tool and how to get set up.

library.title: Write Like Me
library.body:
Write Like Me

What This Is

A prompt designed to help AI models analyze your writing and generate a custom style guide that reflects how you naturally speak and write. It captures your tone, rhythm, phrasing, and personality so your content sounds like you—not a generic AI.

How to Use It

Attach a writing sample—like a video transcript, blog post, or email—that you want the AI to analyze and use as the basis for your style guide.

Aim for at least a few paragraphs, or ideally 1–2 pages of material. The more the AI has to work with, the better it can match your voice.

If you don’t already have a transcript, you can generate one by uploading any video you’ve recorded to a tool like Rev.com, which uses AI and charges based on length, or Descript, which also offers fast transcription.

Once your sample is ready, run the prompt below in ChatGPT (or any large language model) to generate a reusable style guide based on how you communicate.

You can then paste that guide into future prompts—so any AI you use can write in your voice going forward.

		Prompt

				Copy

Study how I communicate in the attached writing sample—pay close attention to my tone, phrasing, rhythm, sentence structure, word choice, and personality.

Then, create a **reusable style guide** that captures how I talk and write. The goal is to make this guide detailed and specific enough that someone else could follow it and reliably write in my voice—regardless of the task or topic.

Avoid vague summaries like “casual” or “professional.” Instead, be specific about the traits you notice. For example, pay attention to:

– **Sentence length and structure**  
– **Tone and formality**  
– **Pacing and rhythm**  
– **Use of transitions**  
– **Favorite phrases or patterns**  
– **Use of emphasis**  
– **Humor and voice quirks**  
– **Call-to-action style**

The final guide should be practical and ready for me to copy and paste into future prompts so the AI consistently writes in my voice.

			Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

From the Session: This prompt was introduced in Video Avatar Seller FAQs.

library.title: Write Like a Human
library.body:
Write Like a Human

What This Is

A writing prompt inspired by Sam Altman, designed to help AI responses sound more like a real person wrote them. It strips out the robotic tone, removes hype, and keeps things direct and conversational—so your content feels natural, clear, and human.

How to use it

Copy and paste the full prompt into any large language model (like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Grok). You can adjust it to fit your style, but the default version works great for making AI sound less like a robot and more like a smart human who knows how to talk like one.

Use this prompt to help with emails, scripts, web copy—anything you want to sound more human and less “AI.”

		Prompt

				Copy

Use the following guidelines to write in a natural, conversational tone:

1. **Use simple, clear language:** Write plainly with short, direct sentences. 
   – Example: “I need help with this issue.”  

2. **Avoid “AI giveaway” phrases:** Skip clichés, robotic wording, or overly polished phrasing. 
   – Avoid: “Let’s dive into this game-changing solution.” 
   – Use instead: “Here’s how it works.”  

3. **Be direct and concise:** Get to the point without filler. 
   – Example: “We should meet tomorrow.”  

4. **Maintain a conversational tone:** Write like you talk. It’s fine to start sentences with “and” or “but.” 
   – Example: “And that’s why it matters.”  

5. **Avoid hype or marketing language:** Keep it real—no over-the-top claims. 
   – Avoid: “This revolutionary product will transform your life.” 
   – Use instead: “This product can help you.”  

6. **Be genuine and honest:** Don’t force a tone. Keep it natural. 
   – Example: “I don’t think that’s the best idea.”  

7. **Simplify grammar if necessary:** Perfect grammar isn’t always needed. Match the context. 
   – Example: “i guess we can try that.”  

8. **Cut the fluff:** Remove unnecessary adjectives, adverbs, or over-explaining. 
   – Example: “We finished the task.”  

9. **Prioritize clarity:** Ensure the message is easy to understand and leaves no room for confusion. 
   – Example: “Please send the file by Monday.”

			Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

Related Topics:

Prompt Like a Pro

   – use instead: “this product can help you.”  

6. **be genuine and honest:** don’t force a tone. keep it natural. 
   – example: “i don’t think that’s the best idea.”  

7. **simplify grammar if necessary:** perfect grammar isn’t always needed. match the context. 
   – example: “i guess we can try that.”  

8. **cut the fluff:** remove unnecessary adjectives, adverbs, or over-explaining. 
   – example: “we finished the task.”  

9. **prioritize clarity:** ensure the message is easy to understand and leaves no room for confusion. 
   – example: “please send the file by monday.”

			show more

library.body_html:
Write Like a Human

What This Is

A writing prompt inspired by Sam Altman, designed to help AI responses sound more like a real person wrote them. It strips out the robotic tone, removes hype, and keeps things direct and conversational—so your content feels natural, clear, and human.

How to use it

Copy and paste the full prompt into any large language model (like ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Grok). You can adjust it to fit your style, but the default version works great for making AI sound less like a robot and more like a smart human who knows how to talk like one.

Use this prompt to help with emails, scripts, web copy—anything you want to sound more human and less “AI.”

Prompt

Copy

Use the following guidelines to write in a natural, conversational tone:

1. Use simple, clear language: Write plainly with short, direct sentences. 
   – Example: “I need help with this issue.”

2. Avoid “AI giveaway” phrases: Skip clichés, robotic wording, or overly polished phrasing. 
   – Avoid: “Let’s dive into this game-changing solution.” 
   – Use instead: “Here’s how it works.”

3. Be direct and concise: Get to the point without filler. 
   – Example: “We should meet tomorrow.”

4. Maintain a conversational tone: Write like you talk. It’s fine to start sentences with “and” or “but.” 
   – Example: “And that’s why it matters.”

5. Avoid hype or marketing language: Keep it real—no over-the-top claims. 
   – Avoid: “This revolutionary product will transform your life.” 
   – Use instead: “This product can help you.”

6. Be genuine and honest: Don’t force a tone. Keep it natural. 
   – Example: “I don’t think that’s the best idea.”

7. Simplify grammar if necessary: Perfect grammar isn’t always needed. Match the context. 
   – Example: “i guess we can try that.”

8. Cut the fluff: Remove unnecessary adjectives, adverbs, or over-explaining. 
   – Example: “We finished the task.”

9. Prioritize clarity: Ensure the message is easy to understand and leaves no room for confusion. 
   – Example: “Please send the file by Monday.”

Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

Prompt Like a Pro

library.title: Year-End Real Estate Recap Infographic
library.body:
Year-End Real Estate Recap Infographic

What This Is

This prompt creates an interactive infographic Artifact inside Claude that tells the story of your local housing market—year by year. It transforms your MLS data into a visual summary of key metrics like median prices, total homes sold, and days on market, all wrapped in your branding and packaged as a portable HTML file.

Use it in email recaps, listing presentations, social content, or client updates. The infographic is self-contained, works offline, and is easy to print or share.

How to use it

Run a year-end MLS search that reflects the data you want to show—typically closed listings in your target area from the past year.

Download the results as a .csv or .txt file, depending on what your MLS supports. This becomes the data source for the infographic.

Prepare a simple brand kit with your logo, font names, and color hex codes. If you don’t have one, just list them in a doc and save it as a PDF or Word file.

Paste the full prompt (below) into Claude, and upload both your data file and brand kit to submit with your prompt.

Claude will generate a fully functional .html file that works offline, includes your branding, and features interactive charts and filters.

Review the output, then share it however you want—email, print, presentation, or embed it on your site. It’s built to make your year-end insights look sharp and credible.

		Prompt

				Copy

You are a globally recognized infographic designer and web developer. Your task is to create a fully self-contained HTML Artifact called the “[Year] [Area] Real Estate Recap.” This infographic should deliver compelling insights and interactive functionality in a format that works seamlessly, even when downloaded. Follow these rules:

**Data Embedding:**
- Use only the MLS data provided in the attached spreadsheet. Embed this data directly into the HTML file using inline JSON or JavaScript.  
- Do not use external data sources or make API calls.

**Libraries and Dependencies:**
- Build interactive charts and graphs (e.g., bar charts, heat maps, line graphs) using embedded JavaScript libraries. Include all dependencies directly in the file to enable offline functionality.

**Content and Features:**
- Highlight key metrics such as median home prices, total homes sold, average days on market, and notable moments from the selected year (e.g., record-breaking sales).  
- Include drop-down menus or sections that allow users to explore specific areas or data points.

**Branding and Styling:**
- Apply consistent visual styling using the attached brand kit, which includes colors (hex codes), font names, and a logo file.  
- If no formal brand kit is provided, use clean, modern design defaults and prompt the user for key visual preferences if needed.

**Output:**
- Provide a downloadable `.html` file that is fully functional offline. Ensure it opens directly in any browser without external dependencies.

The final result should be visually stunning, user-friendly, and entirely portable for offline use.

			Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

Related Topics:

Generating Claude Artifacts

library.title: YouTube Scripts from Seller Search Trends
library.body:
YouTube Scripts from Seller Search Trends

What This Is

This resource provides two structured prompts for creating highly optimized YouTube content that attracts motivated home sellers. You’ll start by generating 50 localized, curiosity-driven video titles based on seller-intent search behavior, then write full 6-minute scripts for selected titles—each one tailored to position you as the go-to listing expert in your market.

This workflow is built around keyword data pulled from AnswerThePublic, which surfaces real questions home sellers are searching for on YouTube. If you use a different keyword research tool, make sure to update the data source fields in the prompt(s) accordingly.

You can either use the resulting script(s) in a teleprompter for on-camera delivery (and may consider using an eye-contact correction tool in post-production—like Captions, Descript, or BIGVU), or you can feed the script to a video avatar generator such as HeyGen or Synthesia for fast, polished delivery without filming.

How To Use It

Step 1: Gather Keyword Data

Go to AnswerThePublic.com and search for a broad term like “sell home.”

Filter the results to show YouTube-related search behavior.

Export the results (Download as spreadsheet).

Step 2: Generate Titles Using Prompt #1

Paste Prompt #1 into your preferred AI tool (e.g. ChatGPT).

Upload/attach your spreadsheet from AnswerThePublic.

You’ll get 50 video title ideas that are short, emotionally engaging, relevant to your local market, and designed to rank on YouTube.

		Prompt

				Copy

# Prompt 1: Writing YouTube Video Titles
You are an experienced YouTube content strategist specializing in real estate and SEO.

## Key Data Points

Consider the following key data points in the completion and customization of this task:

– **Keyword Research Tool:** AnswerThePublic (“Research Tool”)  
– **Primary Keyword (broad):** Sell Home (“Primary Keyword”)  
– **Data Source:** YouTube search behavior via AnswerThePublic (“Data Source”)  
– **Language:** English (“Language”)  
– **Country or Region:** USA (“Region”)  
– **Specified Location (e.g., city, neighborhood, or region):** Location (“Specified Location”)  
– **Optional Style Guide (if provided):**  
  Incorporate the user’s brand voice guide—tone, key phrases, and preferred structure—only if a style guide accompanies the spreadsheet.

## Task Instructions

Review the attached spreadsheet containing keyword data from the **Research Tool**. The data includes searches related to the **Primary Keyword**, specifically reflecting YouTube user behavior in the specified **Language** and **Region**.

### Step 1: Keyword Analysis for YouTube

– Analyze the keyword data with an eye toward **YouTube-specific search behavior**—prioritizing queries that imply the viewer is looking to *watch* something explained, demonstrated, or reviewed visually.  
– Prioritize long-tail phrases that reflect **watch intent** and **topic curiosity**.  
– Emphasize natural-language questions, emotional concerns, or urgent situations home sellers might be searching for on YouTube.  
– Identify emerging concerns or misconceptions that could drive higher engagement and views.  
– **Filter out irrelevant queries by focusing solely on residential real estate.**  
   → Retain only queries that reflect a homeowner’s or seller’s perspective about their primary residence or investment property.  
   → Discard queries related to B2B sales, home-related products (e.g., furniture, decor, cleaning supplies), video games, general e-commerce, or financial product marketing.  
– **Validate spreadsheet structure:**  
   → If any column header or data field is unclear or doesn’t match expected real-estate query, pause generation and ask the user to clarify before proceeding.

### Step 2: Title Ideation and Localization

– **Base each video title on a relevant keyword or search query from the Research Tool.**  
   Titles should reflect the core **intent**, **language**, or **emotional trigger** of the original search phrase.  
– Create **50 YouTube video titles** that are:
  – Short (ideally under 60 characters)  
  – Clickworthy (attention-grabbing but never misleading or clickbait)  
  – Emotionally engaging and curiosity-provoking  
  – Aligned with common seller concerns (e.g., timing the market, getting top dollar, prepping the home, avoiding mistakes)  
  – Phrased to match natural voice or AI-assisted search queries  
  – **Strongly incorporate the Specified Location**—including city, neighborhood, or local terms—to improve visibility and ranking in competitive markets

– Favor titles that begin with strong, curiosity-driven phrases (e.g., “Avoid This Mistake…,” “What Nobody Tells You About…”) to boost engagement.

### Deliverable: 50 Optimized YouTube Titles Targeting Home Sellers

Produce **50 optimized YouTube video titles** that:

– Are directly informed by the provided keyword data  
– Align with YouTube search behavior and suggested video algorithms  
– Reflect natural voice search phrasing and common seller questions, without rigidly mirroring the query structure  
– Prioritize clarity, value, and local relevance over branding or generic phrasing  
– Represent the agent’s tone and authority while maintaining authenticity and audience focus

### Compliance and Integrity

– Adhere to all real estate advertising laws, fair housing guidelines, and ethical standards  
– Avoid overpromising or misleading statements  
– Do not provide financial, legal, tax, or accounting advice—recommend viewers consult licensed professionals  
– Make sure all titles are original, contextually relevant, and aligned with the needs of home sellers in the specified market

			Show more

Step 3: Write a Script Using Prompt #2

Choose one of the titles.

Run Prompt #2 to get a full 6-minute video script.

The script will reference your keyword, location, and brand name.

It’s written in a conversational, second-person style that builds trust with sellers and positions you as the expert.

Step 4: Record or Generate Your Video

Use the script in a teleprompter and film yourself. You can polish it with eye-contact correction tools like Captions, Descript, or BIGVU.

Or skip filming and upload the script to an avatar tool like HeyGen or Synthesia to produce a professional video.

Need help creating your video avatar in HeyGen? It’s all covered in the Video Avatar Seller FAQs lecture: Watch here.

		Prompt

				Copy

# Prompt 2: Writing the YouTube Video Script (Clean Copy Output)
You are an experienced real estate-focused YouTube content writer and strategist.

– **Validate Context:**  
  → Confirm that Primary Keyword, Specified Location, and Agent or Brand Name are defined. If any are missing or unclear, pause and ask the user to clarify before proceeding.  

– **Optional Style Guide (if provided):**  
  Incorporate the user’s brand voice guide—tone, key phrases, and preferred structure—only if a style guide accompanies the context.  

– **Validate Call-to-Action:**  
  → If no specific CTA action (e.g., “call me,” “schedule a chat,” “download guide”) is defined, pause and ask the user what they’d like the viewer to do at the end.  

– **Request MLS Data When Relevant:**  
  → If the video topic involves pricing, timing, market trends, or other data-sensitive insights, pause and ask the user for recent MLS stats before writing the script.

## Task Overview

Your task is to write a 6-minute YouTube video script based on the first of the **50 video titles** generated in Prompt #1.

> **Note:** After completing this first script, the user will manually instruct you to move on to the next title.

Use the following information throughout the script:
- **Primary Keyword** and **Specified Location** from Prompt #1 
- **Agent or Brand Name**, which will be included as part of the provided context

This script will be delivered by the named real estate professional, speaking on camera to local home sellers. It should educate, build trust, and clearly establish their authority as the go-to exper

– Analyze the keyword data with an eye toward YouTube-specific search behavior—prioritizing queries that imply the viewer is looking to *watch* something explained, demonstrated, or reviewed visually.
– Prioritize long-tail phrases that reflect watch intent and topic curiosity.
– Emphasize natural-language questions, emotional concerns, or urgent situations home sellers might be searching for on YouTube.
– Identify emerging concerns or misconceptions that could drive higher engagement and views.
– Filter out irrelevant queries by focusing solely on residential real estate.
→ Retain only queries that reflect a homeowner’s or seller’s perspective about their primary residence or investment property.
→ Discard queries related to B2B sales, home-related products (e.g., furniture, decor, cleaning supplies), video games, general e-commerce, or financial product marketing.
– Validate spreadsheet structure:
→ If any column header or data field is unclear or doesn’t match expected real-estate query, pause generation and ask the user to clarify before proceeding.

### Step 2: Title Ideation and Localization

– Base each video title on a relevant keyword or search query from the Research Tool.
Titles should reflect the core intent, language, or emotional trigger of the original search phrase.
– Create 50 YouTube video titles that are:
– Short (ideally under 60 characters)
– Clickworthy (attention-grabbing but never misleading or clickbait)
– Emotionally engaging and curiosity-provoking
– Aligned with common seller concerns (e.g., timing the market, getting top dollar, prepping the home, avoiding mistakes)
– Phrased to match natural voice or AI-assisted search queries
– Strongly incorporate the Specified Location—including city, neighborhood, or local terms—to improve visibility and ranking in competitive markets

– Favor titles that begin with strong, curiosity-driven phrases (e.g., “Avoid This Mistake…,” “What Nobody Tells You About…”) to boost engagement.

### Deliverable: 50 Optimized YouTube Titles Targeting Home Sellers

Produce 50 optimized YouTube video titles that:

– Are directly informed by the provided keyword data
– Align with YouTube search behavior and suggested video algorithms
– Reflect natural voice search phrasing and common seller questions, without rigidly mirroring the query structure
– Prioritize clarity, value, and local relevance over branding or generic phrasing
– Represent the agent’s tone and authority while maintaining authenticity and audience focus

### Compliance and Integrity

– Adhere to all real estate advertising laws, fair housing guidelines, and ethical standards
– Avoid overpromising or misleading statements
– Do not provide financial, legal, tax, or accounting advice—recommend viewers consult licensed professionals
– Make sure all titles are original, contextually relevant, and aligned with the needs of home sellers in the specified market

Show more

Step 3: Write a Script Using Prompt #2

Choose one of the titles.

Run Prompt #2 to get a full 6-minute video script.

The script will reference your keyword, location, and brand name.

It’s written in a conversational, second-person style that builds trust with sellers and positions you as the expert.

Step 4: Record or Generate Your Video

Use the script in a teleprompter and film yourself. You can polish it with eye-contact correction tools like Captions, Descript, or BIGVU.

Or skip filming and upload the script to an avatar tool like HeyGen or Synthesia to produce a professional video.

Need help creating your video avatar in HeyGen? It’s all covered in the Video Avatar Seller FAQs lecture: Watch here.

Prompt

Copy

# Prompt 2: Writing the YouTube Video Script (Clean Copy Output)
You are an experienced real estate-focused YouTube content writer and strategist.

– Validate Context:
→ Confirm that Primary Keyword, Specified Location, and Agent or Brand Name are defined. If any are missing or unclear, pause and ask the user to clarify before proceeding.

– Optional Style Guide (if provided):
Incorporate the user’s brand voice guide—tone, key phrases, and preferred structure—only if a style guide accompanies the context.

– Validate Call-to-Action:
→ If no specific CTA action (e.g., “call me,” “schedule a chat,” “download guide”) is defined, pause and ask the user what they’d like the viewer to do at the end.

– Request MLS Data When Relevant:
→ If the video topic involves pricing, timing, market trends, or other data-sensitive insights, pause and ask the user for recent MLS stats before writing the script.

## Task Overview

Your task is to write a 6-minute YouTube video script based on the first of the 50 video titles generated in Prompt #1.

> Note: After completing this first script, the user will manually instruct you to move on to the next title.

Use the following information throughout the script:
- Primary Keyword and Specified Location from Prompt #1
- Agent or Brand Name, which will be included as part of the provided context

This script will be delivered by the named real estate professional, speaking on camera to local home sellers. It should educate, build trust, and clearly establish their authority as the go-to exper
library.description: Generate YouTube scripts using researched seller keywords to attract motivated home sellers.

library.title: Zillow ChatGPT Branded Email Announcement
library.body:
Zillow ChatGPT Branded Email Announcement

What This Is

This resource creates a ready-to-send branded email announcing the new connection between Zillow and ChatGPT — written and formatted to match your brand.

The email explains to your database what this Zillow + ChatGPT update means for real estate, why most agents aren’t prepared for it, and how your listings are already positioned to stand out.

You don’t need any design or coding experience — Claude (the AI tool you’ll use for this) handles all of that. It writes the copy, builds the design, applies your logo and brand colors, and gives you a final, copy-and-paste HTML email that’s ready to upload into your email platform.

You’ll just need an email platform that allows you to paste in your own code — this feature is usually called “Paste your own HTML,” “Code your own,” or shown as an icon with <> in your email editor (examples: Mailchimp, ActiveCampaign, SendGrid, Constant Contact).

You don’t have to know how to code. (I don’t.) Claude does that part for you. You’ll simply copy the code it generates and paste it into your email platform — it’ll look like a fully designed, professional email once you preview it.

⚠️ Important: Don’t send this announcement unless you’ve already optimized your listings using the Zillow ChatGPT Listing Optimization Prompt. This email only makes sense if your listings have been written and optimized through that process. Otherwise, it’s making a promise your marketing doesn’t yet deliver.

How to Use This

Copy the prompt below by clicking the clipboard icon in the top-right corner of this resource.

Open Claude (recommended — Claude writes better code and designs better emails).

You can use ChatGPT, but Claude consistently produces the cleanest, most reliable HTML for this kind of task.

Start a new chat in Claude.

If the prompt appears in the message box below your input (i.e., where you type), just type: “Follow the instructions attached.” (The prompt is very long.) Then submit. Claude will automatically begin walking you through the setup.

Answer the setup questions. They’ll appear one at a time. Here’s what to expect:

Email platform: The system you use to send marketing emails (Mailchimp, Constant Contact, etc.).

Reply-to email: Where replies should go if someone clicks “Reply.”

Brand colors: You can list colors by name (“Navy and Gold”) or use hex codes like #1C4C8A.

Logo or header image URL: This must be a link, not an upload. Claude can’t use attached files. You’ll want to grab the URL where your logo or header image is already hosted — usually inside your email platform’s image library. For example, in Mailchimp or ActiveCampaign, you can upload your image and then copy the direct link.

Fonts: Either specify or simply say “you decide” and Claude will choose clean, modern fonts that work across devices.

Rounded corners: Refers to whether your email container and buttons have soft, curved edges or squared ones.

CTA (call-to-action): What you want readers to do — for example, “Schedule a Call,” “Reply to This Email,” or “View Listings.”

Wait a moment while Claude builds your email.

It will write your copy, apply your colors, and format everything in HTML.

Copy the finished HTML code.

This is your full email — both the text and the visual layout (colors, fonts, buttons, etc.).

Paste it into your email platform.

Look for options like “Code your own,” “Paste HTML,” or the <> icon.

In Mailchimp: click “Design Email” → “Code Your Own” → “Paste in Code.”

In ActiveCampaign or SendGrid: switch to the HTML or Custom Code editor.

Once pasted, preview the email and send yourself a test to confirm it looks right on both desktop and mobile.

Make edits if needed.

Ask Claude to adjust tone, colors, spacing, or button style. Example: “Can you make the headline text darker?” or “Can you center the button?”

Add your subject line and preheader text.

Claude will ask if you’d like it to generate one for you. Keep both short, conversational, and sentence-case.

Send your email.

Once you’ve tested and reviewed it, send or schedule your campaign in your email platform.

If you plan to reuse this prompt later, save your answers somewhere so you can paste them back in next time.

💡 Quick Tips

You don’t need to understand HTML — it’s just the code that controls how your email looks.

Your email platform must support “paste your own HTML.” If it doesn’t, this process won’t work.

Keep color usage simple: one main accent color for buttons or headers.

The first two lines (“Zillow is now built into ChatGPT.” and “Real estate just changed—again.”) should be bold but the same size as the rest of the text.

If your test email looks off, ask Claude to “fix layout for [Mailchimp/ActiveCampaign/etc.].”

When finished, your branded announcement email will:

Clearly explain the Zillow + ChatGPT update,

Position your listings as AI-optimized, and

Reflect your exact brand colors, fonts, and tone.

🧩 Pair this with: Zillow ChatGPT Listing Optimization Prompt. This is the foundation that makes this email true. Complete that first, then send this announcement to your database.

		Prompt

				Copy

# ZILLOW + CHATGPT ANNOUNCEMENT EMAIL GENERATOR

You are an expert email copywriter and designer specializing in branded HTML emails for real estate professionals. Your job is to generate a copy/paste-ready announcement email about Zillow's integration with ChatGPT, customized to the agent's brand and market.

## INTAKE QUESTIONS

**IMPORTANT:** When displaying these questions to the user, format them with clear line breaks between each question for easy readability.

Please answer all of the following questions. You can use Shift + Enter to create line breaks between your answers.

**PLATFORM & DELIVERY:**

1. What email service provider (ESP) do you use? (Mailchimp, Constant Contact, ActiveCampaign, SendGrid, or other?)

2. What is your reply-to email address? (for people who want to reply)

**AGENT/TEAM INFO:**

3. What is your name or team name? (for the signature)

4. Are you a solo agent or a team? (affects pronouns: "I/my" vs "we/our")

5. What is your primary market focus? (single-family homes, condos, luxury homes, etc.)

**LOCATION/MARKET:**

6. What city/region do you serve? (this will replace location references in the email)

7. What are 2 specific neighborhoods or areas in your market? (these will replace "Franklin" and "Brentwood" in the examples)

**BRANDING:**

8. **Brand colors:** Provide your brand color palette. You can share 2-6 colors with their hex codes. For example:
   - Simple: "Teal #31DBA5, Navy #1C4C8A"
   - Complete palette: "Primary: #31DBA5, Secondary: #1C4C8A, Tertiary: #E09F3E, Gray: #A4ACBA, Black: #2D323C, White: #F9F9F9"
   
   *If you only know color names (like "Gold" or "Navy Blue"), just tell me and I'll suggest hex codes for your approval.*

**LOGO/HEADER:**

9. Logo or header image URL:

10. Is this a logo or a full header graphic?
 - If **logo**: What color is your logo? (white, black, full color, etc.)
 - If **full header graphic**: Skip to question 12

11. If you have a logo (not a full header), what background should it sit on? 
 - Solid color (provide hex code, e.g., #31DBA5)
 - Gradient (I'll use your brand colors)
 - White background
 - No background (transparent)

**DESIGN PREFERENCES:**

12. Do you want rounded corners on the email container? (Yes/No - default is yes with 16px radius)

13. Button style preference:
 - Gradient (uses primary and secondary colors)
 - Solid color (uses primary color only)
 - Simple (solid color with border, no fill)

**FONTS:**

14. Primary font preference (or type "default" for web-safe fonts):

15. Secondary font preference (or type "default" for web-safe fonts):

**SIGNATURE:**

16. What should appear in your email signature? Include: Name, Title/Role, Phone Number (and anything else you want)

**CALL-TO-ACTION:**

Choose one of the following:
- **Option 1:** Schedule a call/consultation (provide your scheduling link URL, or type "mailto" to use email reply)
- **O

library.title: Zillow ChatGPT Listing Optimization Prompt
library.body:
Zillow ChatGPT Listing Optimization Prompt

What This Is

This resource helps real-estate listing agents write AI-search-optimized MLS listing descriptions that perform better inside ChatGPT’s new Zillow integration.

Through OpenAI’s new Apps SDK, buyers can now search Zillow directly in ChatGPT using natural-language prompts such as:

“Show me homes with sunset views in Franklin”“Homes near coffee shops in Nashville, TN”

When someone runs a search like that, ChatGPT sends Zillow a structured search request (region + keywords + filters).

If a landmark is mentioned—like a specific Starbucks—ChatGPT translates that place into map coordinates and Zillow returns homes closest to that location. Zillow then performs a fuzzy full-text search across its data, including the Public Remarks, address, neighborhood names, and community metadata. That means your remarks don’t need exact phrase matches—but strong, factual keywords and recognizable place names improve how often your listing appears.

Zillow never shares or displays your remarks text in ChatGPT—it only uses that text behind the scenes to decide which listings show up. Photo captions, agent notes, and media text don’t sync at all, so the Public Remarks remain the single most influential searchable field.

This prompt (or the included Custom GPT instructions) guides ChatGPT to:

Ask you for a property address and key details.

Automatically search prior listings and nearby amenities.

Generate a keyword-rich, MLS-compliant Public Remarks section optimized for ChatGPT’s Zillow search ranking.

Keep everything fully aligned with Fair Housing, RESPA, and equivalent housing laws.NOTE: You should always doublecheck compliance before publishing. AI makes mistakes.

In short, this resource helps your listings stand out more often in ChatGPT’s Zillow results—based on how buyers actually search for homes like yours.

How to Use This

You can use this resource two ways—either as a one-time prompt or by setting up your own Custom GPT.

Option 1: Use the Modular Prompt

Copy the entire prompt from this resource.

Paste it into a new chat in ChatGPT (recommended).

It can also run in tools like Claude or Gemini, but ChatGPT is best because it connects directly to Zillow and preserves formatting.

Start a new chat before pasting to avoid unrelated context.

Submit the prompt exactly as written and press Enter.

ChatGPT will introduce itself as your Listing Optimization Assistant.

Follow the on-screen intake (Phase 1).

You’ll provide the property address or a prior/draft listing URL.

It will automatically search major portals (Zillow, Realtor.com, Redfin) for existing data.

You can optionally upload photos, floor plans, or feature sheets.

It will then scan the area for nearby amenities like parks, shops, and trails.

Review the checklist it returns.

Confirm which features are accurate, missing, or need editing.

Provide your MLS character limit.

ChatGPT will tailor the remarks length precisely.

Review your finished Public Remarks.

You’ll receive the optimized text, character count, and a Nearby Amenity List.

Copy and paste the remarks into your MLS.

They’ll be factual, keyword-dense, and Fair Housing-compliant.

Pro Tip: Replace vague phrasing like “walkable” with factual distances (e.g., “0.3 mi to Sevier Park”). Avoid any language implying demographics or preferences.

		Prompt

				Copy

You are a real estate **listing optimization assistant** for **listing agents**. 

Zillow is now integrated into ChatGPT through OpenAI’s **Apps SDK** program. 
Homebuyers can now search conversationally inside ChatGPT using natural language queries such as: 
- “Show me homes with sunset views in [LOCATION].” 
- “Homes nearby to coffee shops in [LOCATION].” 
- “Single-story homes with fenced yards in [LOCATION].” 

When a user types a query like this, ChatGPT sends Zillow a **structured search request** containing the region, keyword(s), and any optional filters. If a landmark is mentioned (like a specific Starbucks or park), ChatGPT converts that place into **latitude and longitude coordinates**, and Zillow returns listings **closest to that location**. 

Zillow then performs a **fuzzy, full-text search** across its indexed listing data, including: 
- **Public Remarks** (the agent’s written description) 
- **Address and street names** 
- **Neighborhood or subdivision names** 
- **Community metadata** (e.g., “walkable,” “gated,” “sidewalk-lined”) 
- **Geographic proximity** to any mapped landmarks 

The results that appear in ChatGPT are **property cards only** (address, price, beds, baths, etc.). 
Zillow uses the Public Remarks text for **keyword relevance and ranking**, but **the text itself is never shown** due to MLS copyright restrictions. 
**Photo captions, agent notes, and other media text do not sync** to ChatGPT or Zillow’s API. 

Because of that, Zillow’s ranking in ChatGPT depends heavily on what’s written in the Public Remarks. 
If an agent doesn’t mention a feature (like sunset views or proximity to coffee shops), Zillow’s fuzzy search can’t prioritize the listing for those keywords — and ChatGPT won’t surface it in that kind of query. 

Your role is to help the **listing agent** optimize the **Public Remarks** section of their MLS listing so that: 
1. The property appears more often when buyers use natural-language searches inside ChatGPT. 
2. The text provides Zillow’s algorithm with clear, factual, keyword-rich cues for ranking and relevance. 
3. The remarks remain fully compliant with MLS rules, **Fair Housing**, and **RESPA**. 

---

### ⚙️ OBJECTIVE
Create a fact-based, keyword-rich, MLS-compliant **Public Remarks** that maximize visibility in ChatGPT’s Zillow-powered searches. 
You will: 
1. Collect property details and available assets. 
2. Automatically perform reconnaissance on nearby amenities and listing media. 
3. Confirm findings with the agent. 
4. Adapt to the agent’s MLS character limits. 
5. Write clear, efficient, and compliant listing remarks packed with factual details Zillow can match to buyer searches. 

---

### 🧱 COMPLIANCE GUARDRAILS
- Always comply with the **Fair Housing Act** and **RESPA** (or equivalent local housing laws). 
- Never use or imply language about demographics, families, safety, religion, national origin, age, disability, or any protected class. 
- Use **objective, factual, place-based** statements only (e.g., “0.5 mi to Sevier Park,” not “great for families”). 
- Avoid adjectives implying preference or exclusivity (“exclusive,” “safe,” “family-friendly”). 
- Use **concise, factual nouns** over adjectives to maximize keyword efficiency. 
- **Do not include** the listing agent’s name, contact info, or brokerage. Zillow prohibits this. 
- Avoid “walkable” or “within walking distance”; instead, use **distance-based phrasing** (e.g., “0.3 miles to Sevier Park”). 

---

### 🧭 PHASE 1: INTAKE + AUTO RECON

Start by introducing yourself naturally:  
> “I’ll help you optimize your MLS Public Remarks so your listing performs better in ChatGPT’s Zillow searches. I’ll ask a few quick questions, then I’ll pull in data and return a checklist for you to confirm.”  

Then proceed step-by-step:  
1. Ask:  
   > “What’s the **property address** or a **listing URL** (Zillow, MLS, or brokerage site) I can reference?”  
   - If an address only is provided, automatically **search Zillow and major listing portals** (Realtor.com, Redfin, brokerage sites) for any current or prior listing.  
   - If found, **parse** the page for features such as pool, EV charger, upgrades (with years), floor plan, and media types.  
   - If nothing is found, continue using any uploads the agent provides.  

2. Ask:  
   > “Which **MLS** are you using, and what’s your exact **character limit** for Public Remarks?”  

3. Ask:  
   > “Do you want to upload any **photos, floor plans, or feature sheets** to help me verify property details?”  

4. Ask:  
   > “Does the Zillow page include a **3D tour, video, or floor plan viewer**? Those don’t sync to ChatGPT, so I’ll need to reference them in the remarks.”  

After collecting inputs

library.title: 10 Formatting Tips for Better Prompting
library.body:
10 Formatting Tips for Better Prompting

Introduction

The way you format your prompts when communicating with advanced AI language models like ChatGPT, Claude, Grok, or Gemini significantly impacts the quality of responses you receive. Much like effective human communication, well-structured prompts help AI systems better understand your intentions and generate more relevant, useful outputs.

This guide presents ten proven formatting techniques to enhance your AI interactions and get more precise, helpful responses.

1. Use Line Breaks for Clarity

Breaking your requests into separate lines helps the AI process each instruction individually rather than as a dense paragraph.

Instead of writing:

"Please write social media captions for my new luxury property listing, suggest email subject lines I could use when sending it to my client database, and draft a property description for the MLS listing."

Try formatting it as:

"Please help with the following for my new luxury property listing:

Write 3 engaging social media captions I can use with listing photos.

Suggest 5 email subject lines to capture attention in my client newsletter.

Draft a compelling property description for the MLS listing."

When you place each request on its own line, the AI can focus on one task at a time, producing better quality content for each marketing need.

2. Use Bullet Points or Numbered Lists

When your prompt contains a series of items, criteria, or questions, formatting them as a clearly delineated list helps the AI identify and address each point separately.

For unordered collections, use bullet points (- or *):

Please brainstorm content ideas for my real estate newsletter:
- Market update for the spring season
- Home staging tips for sellers
- Current mortgage rate information
- Local neighborhood spotlight

For sequential items, use numbers:

Help me create a follow-up sequence for potential buyers:
1. Write an initial email after the first showing
2. Draft a text message to send 2 days later
3. Create a personalized note about neighborhood amenities
4. Develop a 30-day check-in message

This approach ensures you get complete responses addressing each specific marketing need you’ve outlined.

3. Wrap Example Inputs in Quotes or Code Blocks

When including sample text for the AI to analyze, rewrite, or use as inspiration, set it apart from your instructions using quotation marks or code blocks.

Using quotation marks:

"Rewrite this property headline to be more engaging: 'Beautiful 3BR/2BA home in desirable neighborhood'"

Using code blocks with triple backticks:

When you type three backticks, then add your text, then close with three more backticks, it creates a code block like this:

Here's my current agent bio that needs improvement:

Jane Smith has been a real estate agent for 15 years. She knows the local market well and has helped many clients buy and sell homes. Jane lives in Oakwood with her family and enjoys cooking and hiking.

This visual separation clearly distinguishes between your instructions and the content you want the AI to work with, resulting in more targeted improvements to your marketing copy.

4. Use Markdown for Structure

Most AI platforms support Markdown, a simple formatting system that adds visual hierarchy and emphasis to plain text.

Headers and subheaders (use # symbols):

# Quarterly Market Report Email

## Current Market Trends
Key statistics about home prices in our region...

## Buyer Opportunities
Tips for buyers in the current market...

## Seller Strategies
Best practices for preparing homes for sale...

Text formatting:

Bold text (use **bold text**)

Italic text (use *italic text*)

Special terms (use backticks `term`)

These formatting elements help organize complex prompts when planning comprehensive marketing campaigns or detailed client presentations.

5. Separate Multi-Step Requests

For prompts that contain multiple questions or tasks, use double line breaks to visually separate each component:

I need help creating marketing materials for a new luxury listing. 

Can you draft social media copy that highlights the property's custom kitchen and resort-style pool?

What would be an effective headline for the property brochure that emphasizes its exclusive location?

How should I describe the neighborhood amenities in my email campaign to potential buyers?

This approach makes your request easier to follow and allows the AI to address each marketing element thoroughly.

6. Keep Each Instruction on Its Own Line

Placing individual instructions on separate lines improves clarity and comprehension:

Instead of:

"Write a property description for my new listing highlighting the renovated kitchen, mention the hardwood floors, talk about the large backyard, and emphasize the great school district."

Try:

"Please write a property description for my new listing.
Highlight the recently renovated kitchen with quartz countertops.
Mention the original hardwood floors throughout the main level.
Describe the spacious fenced backyard with mature landscaping.
Emphasize the property's location in the award-winning Lincoln school district."

This vertical organization ensures all key selling points are incorporated into your property descriptions and marketing materials.

7. Use Delimiters for Sections

For longer, complex prompts, use visual delimiters like dashes, equals signs, or symbols to create clearly labeled sections:

=== PROPERTY DETAILS ===
4BR/3BA Colonial in Lakewood Heights
2,400 sq ft on 0.5 acre lot
Built in 2005, renovated in 2022
Asking price: $575,000

--- TARGET AUDIENCE ---
* First-time luxury buyers
* Growing families looking for more space
* Professionals who work from home

+++ MARKETING REQUESTS +++
What headline would capture attention on social media?
How should we highlight the home office space for remote workers?
What unique selling points should be emphasized in the property flyer?

These visual dividers help organize related information and create a clear structure for the AI to follow when creating your marketing strategy.

8. SpecifyDesired Formats or Templates

Clearly indicate the format you want for the AI’s response:

Please create a monthly content calendar for my real estate social media using this template:

WEEK 1:
Monday: [Topic]
Wednesday: [Topic]
Friday: [Topic]

WEEK 2:
Monday: [Topic]
Wednesday: [Topic]
Friday: [Topic]

Focus on spring home selling tips, local market updates, and buyer financing options.

Providing a specific structure ensures your social media or email content will be formatted exactly as you need it for your marketing platforms.

9. Provide Context Before Questions

Share relevant background information before stating your specific requests:

I'm a real estate agent specializing in luxury waterfront properties in the Naples area. My typical clients are retirees or vacation home buyers looking for exclusive amenities and views.

What key selling points should I emphasize in my marketing materials to appeal to this demographic? How can I structure my property descriptions to highlight the lifestyle benefits of these homes?

Giving context first helps the AI understand your target market and provide more relevant, tailored marketing advice for your specific niche.

10. Use Explicit Role Instructions

Define the perspective or expertise you want the AI to adopt:

As an experienced real estate copywriter, please review this draft of my Just Listed postcard and suggest specific improvements:

[Your postcard copy here]

This technique helps frame the AI’s response through the lens of real estate marketing expertise, providing you with professional-quality suggestions and improvements.

Conclusion

These ten formatting techniques will help you craft clearer prompts that elicit more precise, useful responses from AI language models. The extra few moments spent structuring your requests can significantly improve the quality of the AI’s output for your marketing materials, saving you time and enhancing your communications.

Remember that effective prompt engineering is both an art and a science—experiment with these techniques

breaking your requests into separate lines helps the ai process each instruction individually rather than as a dense paragraph.

instead of writing:

"please write social media captions for my new luxury property listing, suggest email subject lines i could use when sending it to my client database, and draft a property description for the mls listing."

try formatting it as:

"please help with the following for my new luxury property listing:

write 3 engaging social media captions i can use with listing photos.

suggest 5 email subject lines to capture attention in my client newsletter.

draft a compelling property description for the mls listing."

when you place each request on its own line, the ai can focus on one task at a time, producing better quality content for each marketing need.

2. use bullet points or numbered lists

when your prompt contains a series of items, criteria, or questions, formatting them as a clearly delineated list helps the ai identify and address each point separately.

for unordered collections, use bullet points (- or *):

please brainstorm content ideas for my real estate newsletter:
- market update for the spring season
- home staging tips for sellers
- current mortgage rate information
- local neighborhood spotlight

for sequential items, use numbers:

help me create a follow-up sequence for potential buyers:
1. write an initial email after the first showing
2. draft a text message to send 2 days later
3. create a personalized note about neighborhood amenities
4. develop a 30-day check-in message

this approach ensures you get complete responses addressing each specific marketing need you’ve outlined.

3. wrap example inputs in quotes or code blocks

when including sample text for the ai to analyze, rewrite, or use as inspiration, set it apart from your instructions using quotation marks or code blocks.

using quotation marks:

"rewrite this property headline to be more engaging: 'beautiful 3br/2ba home in desirable neighborhood'"

using code blocks with triple backticks:

when you type three backticks, then add your text, then close with three more backticks, it creates a code block like this:

here's my current agent bio that needs improvement:

jane smith has been a real estate agent for 15 years. she knows the local market well and has helped many clients buy and sell homes. jane lives in oakwood with her family and enjoys cooking and hiking.

this visual separation clearly distinguishes between your instructions and the content you want the ai to work with, resulting in more targeted improvements to your marketing copy.

4. use markdown for structure

most ai platforms support markdown, a simple formatting system that adds visual hierarchy and emphasis to plain text.

headers and subheaders (use # symbols):

# quarterly market report email

## current market trends
key statistics about home prices in our region...

## buyer opportunities
tips for buyers in the current market...

## seller strategies
best practices for preparing homes for sale...

text formatting:

bold text (use **bold text**)

italic text (use *italic text*)

special terms (use backticks `term`)

these formatting elements help organize complex prompts when planning comprehensive marketing campaigns or detailed client presentations.

5. separate multi-step requests

for prompts that contain multiple questions or tasks, use double line breaks to visually separate each component:

i need help creating marketing materials for a new luxury listing. 

can you draft social media copy that highlights the property's custom kitchen and resort-style pool?

what would be an effective headline for the property brochure that emphasizes its exclusive location?

how should i describe the neighborhood amenities in my email campaign to potential buyers?

this approach makes your request easier to follow and allows the ai to address each marketing element thoroughly.

6. keep each instruction on its own line

placing individual instructions on separate lines improves clarity and comprehension:

instead of:

"write a property description for my new listing highlighting the renovated kitchen, mention the hardwood floors, talk about the large backyard, and emphasize the great school district."

try:

"please write a property description for my new listing.
highlight the recently renovated kitchen with quartz countertops.
mention the original hardwood floors throughout the main level.
describe the spacious fenced backyard with mature landscaping.
emphasize the property's location in the award-winning lincoln school district."

this vertical organization ensures all key selling points are incorporated into your property descriptions and marketing materials.

7. use delimiters for sections

for longer, complex prompts, use visual delimiters like dashes, equals signs, or symbols to create clearly labeled sections:

=== property details ===
4br/3ba colonial in lakewood heights
2,400 sq ft on 0.5 acre lot
built in 2005, renovated in 2022
asking price: $575,000

--- target audience ---
* first-time luxury buyers
* growing families looking for more space
* professionals who work from home

+++ marketing requests +++
what headline would capture attention on social media?
how should we highlight the home office space for remote workers?
what unique selling points should be emphasized in the property flyer?

these visual dividers help organize related information and create a clear structure for the ai to follow when creating your marketing strategy.

8. specifydesired formats or templates

clearly indicate the format you want for the ai’s response:

please create a monthly content calendar for my real estate social media using this template:

week 1:
monday: [topic]
wednesday: [topic]
friday: [topic]

week 2:
monday: [topic]
wednesday: [topic]
friday: [topic]

focus on spring home selling tips, local market updates, and buyer financing options.

providing a specific structure ensures your social media or email content will be formatted exactly as you need it for your marketing platforms.

9. provide context before questions

share relevant background information before stating your specific requests:

i'm a real estate agent specializing in luxury waterfront properties in the naples area. my typical clients are retirees or vacation home buyers looking for exclusive amenities and views.

what key selling points should i emphasize in my marketing materials to appeal to this demographic? how can i structure my property descriptions to highlight the lifestyle benefits of these homes?

giving context first helps the ai understand your target market and provide more relevant, tailored marketing advice for your specific niche.

10. use explicit role instructions

define the perspective or expertise you want the ai to adopt:

as an experienced real estate copywriter, please review this draft of my just listed postcard and suggest specific improvements:

[your postcard copy here]

this technique helps frame the ai’s response through the lens of real estate marketing expertise, providing you with professional-quality suggestions and improvements.

conclusion

these ten formatting techniques will help you craft clearer prompts that elicit more precise, useful responses from ai language models. the extra few moments spent structuring your requests can significantly improve the quality of the ai’s output for your marketing materials, saving you time and enhancing your communications.

10 Formatting Tips for Better Prompting

Introduction

The way you format your prompts when communicating with advanced AI language models like ChatGPT, Claude, Grok, or Gemini significantly impacts the quality of responses you receive. Much like effective human communication, well-structured prompts help AI systems better understand your intentions and generate more relevant, useful outputs.

This guide presents ten proven formatting techniques to enhance your AI interactions and get more precise, helpful responses.

  1. Use Line Breaks for Clarity

Breaking your requests into separate lines helps the AI process each instruction individually rather than as a dense paragraph.

Instead of writing:

"Please write social media captions for my new luxury property listing, suggest email subject lines I could use when sending it to my client database, and draft a property description for the MLS listing."

Try formatting it as:

"Please help with the following for my new luxury property listing:

Write 3 engaging social media captions I can use with listing photos.

Suggest 5 email subject lines to capture attention in my client newsletter.

Draft a compelling property description for the MLS listing."

When you place each request on its own line, the AI can focus on one task at a time, producing better quality content for each marketing need.

  1. Use Bullet Points or Numbered Lists

When your prompt contains a series of items, criteria, or questions, formatting them as a clearly delineated list helps the AI identify and address each point separately.

For unordered collections, use bullet points (- or *):

Please brainstorm content ideas for my real estate newsletter:
- Market update for the spring season
- Home staging tips for sellers
- Current mortgage rate information
- Local neighborhood spotlight

For sequential items, use numbers:

Help me create a follow-up sequence for potential buyers:
1. Write an initial email after the first showing
2. Draft a text message to send 2 days later
3. Create a personalized note about neighborhood amenities
4. Develop a 30-day check-in message

This approach ensures you get complete responses addressing each specific marketing need you’ve outlined.

  1. Wrap Example Inputs in Quotes or Code Blocks

When including sample text for the AI to analyze, rewrite, or use as inspiration, set it apart from your instructions using quotation marks or code blocks.

Using quotation marks:

"Rewrite this property headline to be more engaging: 'Beautiful 3BR/2BA home in desirable neighborhood'"

Using code blocks with triple backticks:

When you type three backticks, then add your text, then close with three more backticks, it creates a code block like this:

Here's my current agent bio that needs improvement:

Jane Smith has been a real estate agent for 15 years. She knows the local market well and has helped many clients buy and sell homes. Jane lives in Oakwood with her family and enjoys cooking and hiking.

This visual separation clearly distinguishes between your instructions and the content you want the AI to work with, resulting in more targeted improvements to your marketing copy.

  1. Use Markdown for Structure

Most AI platforms support Markdown, a simple formatting system that adds visual hierarchy and emphasis to plain text.

Headers and subheaders (use # symbols):

# Quarterly Market Report Email

## Current Market Trends
Key statistics about home prices in our region...

## Buyer Opportunities
Tips for buyers in the current market...

## Seller Strategies
Best practices for preparing homes for sale...

Text formatting:

Bold text (use bold text)

Italic text (use *italic text*)

Special terms (use backticks term)

These formatting elements help organize complex prompts when planning comprehensive marketing campaigns or detailed client presentations.

  1. Separate Multi-Step Requests

For prompts that contain multiple questions or tasks, use double line breaks to visually separate each component:

I need help creating marketing materials for a new luxury listing.

Can you draft social media copy that highlights the property's custom kitchen and resort-style pool?

What would be an effective headline for the property brochure that emphasizes its exclusive location?

How should I describe the neighborhood amenities in my email campaign to potential buyers?

This approach makes your request easier to follow and allows the AI to address each marketing element thoroughly.

  1. Keep Each Instruction on Its Own Line

Placing individual instructions on separate lines improves clarity and comprehension:

Instead of:

"Write a property description for my new listing highlighting the renovated kitchen, mention the hardwood floors, talk about the large backyard, and emphasize the great school district."

Try:

"Please write a property description for my new listing.
Highlight the recently renovated kitchen with quartz countertops.
Mention the original hardwood floors throughout the main level.
Describe the spacious fenced backyard with mature landscaping.
Emphasize the property's location in the award-winning Lincoln school district."

This vertical organization ensures all key selling points are incorporated into your property descriptions and marketing materials.

  1. Use Delimiters for Sections

For longer, complex prompts, use visual delimiters like dashes, equals signs, or symbols to create clearly labeled sections:

=== PROPERTY DETAILS ===
4BR/3BA Colonial in Lakewood Heights
2,400 sq ft on 0.5 acre lot
Built in 2005, renovated in 2022
Asking price: $575,000

--- TARGET AUDIENCE ---
* First-time luxury buyers
* Growing families looking for more space
* Professionals who work from home

+++ MARKETING REQUESTS +++
What headline would capture attention on social media?
How should we highlight the home office space for remote workers?
What unique selling points should be emphasized in the property flyer?

These visual dividers help organize related information and create a clear structure for the AI to follow when creating your marketing strategy.

  1. SpecifyDesired Formats or Templates

Clearly indicate the format you want for the AI’s response:

Please create a monthly content calendar for my real estate social media using this template:

WEEK 1:
Monday: [Topic]
Wednesday: [Topic]
Friday: [Topic]

WEEK 2:
Monday: [Topic]
Wednesday: [Topic]
Friday: [Topic]

Focus on spring home selling tips, local market updates, and buyer financing options.

Providing a specific structure ensures your social media or email content will be formatted exactly as you need it for your marketing platforms.

  1. Provide Context Before Questions

Share relevant background information before stating your specific requests:

I'm a real estate agent specializing in luxury waterfront properties in the Naples area. My typical clients are retirees or vacation home buyers looking for exclusive amenities and views.

What key selling points should I emphasize in my marketing materials to appeal to this demographic? How can I structure my property descriptions to highlight the lifestyle benefits of these homes?

Giving context first helps the AI understand your target market and provide more relevant, tailored marketing advice for your specific niche.

  1. Use Explicit Role Instructions

Define the perspective or expertise you want the AI to adopt:

As an experienced real estate copywriter, please review this draft of my Just Listed postcard and suggest specific improvements:

[Your postcard copy here]

This technique helps frame the AI’s response through the lens of real estate marketing expertise, providing you with professional-quality suggestions and improvements.

Conclusion

These ten formatting techniques will help you craft clearer prompts that elicit more precise, useful responses from AI language models. The extra few moments spent structuring your requests can significantly improve the quality of the AI’s output for your marketing materials, saving you time and enhancing your communications.

Remember that effective prompt engineering is both an art and a science—experiment with these techniques

library.title: 26 AI Tools Heading Into 2026
library.body:
26 AI Tools Heading Into 2026

One of the most common questions I get is some version of “What AI tools do you use or recommend?”

This list is my answer—26 tools I either rely on daily or actively test. The first section covers my core stack: the tools that power my workflow. The second section highlights tools worth experimenting with, depending on what you’re working on.

Use this as a starting point. You don’t need all of these—nobody does. But knowing what’s out there (and what each tool does well) helps you make smarter choices about where to invest your time and money.

Core Stack (Tools I Rely On)

1. ChatGPT – Still my default for thinking things through, writing, voice dictation, and everyday problem-solving. Projects and custom GPTs make it far more useful and nuanced than a blank chat window. Plus its built-in App Store to connect tools like Mailchimp and Adobe.

2. Claude – When I’m working with data, creating files like docs or spreadsheets, or writing code (e.g., HTML for email campaigns), this is where I go to get it done intelligently.

3. Gemini – Nano Banana Pro for image and graphic design, plus Veo 3.1 for short video generation, are top-tier. It rivals ChatGPT for general use and is built into Google’s ecosystem of apps.

4. Perplexity – My go-to for deep research. And its Tasks feature lets you run recurring research automatically—similar to Google Alerts, but smarter.

5. Canva – Still the fastest way to produce real design assets like slides or social graphics. Its AI editing capabilities are super solid.

6. CapCut – The video editor I use most often. It adds (useful) AI assistance without taking control away from you.

7. Captions – Especially strong for subtitles and AI-assisted edits. I use it alongside CapCut, not instead of it.

8. Comet – Perplexity’s agentic browser. “Agentic” here means you can give it instructions and it will actually control the browser for you—clicking, navigating, and completing tasks on your behalf.

9. n8n – Not beginner-friendly, but extremely powerful. This is how you build real automation inside your business—connecting systems and bringing your marketing and operations to life without manual work.

10. Zapier Agents – Much easier than n8n. You give an agent clear instructions (a prompt), and it handles the steps across your apps without you building every step manually. Copilot (built in) can configure the agent for you.

11. NotebookLM – Excellent for learning. You feed it your sources (links or pasted text), and it stays grounded in that material while creating summaries you can chat with, podcast-style audio overviews, infographics, or reports.

12. ElevenLabs – The best voice cloning I’ve used. The “Pro” clone is worth it if you’re podcasting or recording voiceovers for videos.

13. HeyGen – The closest thing I’ve seen to a usable video avatar of yourself, especially when paired with audio from ElevenLabs.

Worth Testing (Useful, Not Essential)

14. Otterly.ai – Helps you monitor and improve how you appear inside AI-driven search and answer engines.

15. ManyChat – Still the best tool for Instagram DMs. The addition of AI lets conversations go deeper and feel more natural, instead of being limited to prewritten scripts.

16. Descript – A strong video editor with the best transcription-based editing I’ve seen. You can edit video by editing the text.

17. Sora – OpenAI’s video-focused social platform. Early and imperfect, but interesting for experimenting with short-form video.

18. Atlas – OpenAI’s contender in agentic browsing. Chromium-based, but not as capable as Comet in my experience.

19. Gamma – A fast way to create slide presentations, though I still default to Canva or Gemini for most production work.

20. Lovable – Part of the “vibe coding” category. You describe what you want, and it builds websites or simple apps without needing to code.

21. LumaLabs – Quality video generation, but what really stands out is Interactive Scenes, which let you turn a video you record (like walking around a home) into an explorable 3D model you can embed or export.

22. Higgsfield – A creator-friendly video platform with guided options and presets for trending social videos, instead of starting from a blank prompt.

23. RunwayML – Known for realistic video generation and the ability to keep the same character consistent across multiple scenes.

24. PLAUD – A small physical recorder you bring to meetings. It records conversations and uses AI afterward to summarize, analyze, and help you remember what was said and promised.

25. Artlist.io – Longtime source for music and b-roll, now also lets you generate video using top AI models like Veo, Sora, and Kling.

26. Wisprflow – A dictation app that lets you speak out loud and have your words appear directly inside any app on your computer.

Want More?

Check out the Tools to Test section on your main profile page. I keep an updated list there of AI tools organized by use case—so you can quickly find what you need based on what you’re trying to accomplish.

Related Topics:

Smart, Savvy AI Spending
