library.title: 7 Magic Phrases That Make Prompts Better
library.body:
7 Magic Phrases That Make Prompts Better

What This Is

This resource gives you a tactical list of prompt-enhancing phrases that consistently trigger better thinking, structure, or tone from AI models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini.

These aren’t just random suggestions. Each phrase is backed by behavior observed in large language models. They help the model pause, plan, clarify, or write with more focus—leading to cleaner outputs with less back-and-forth.

You can insert them directly into your own prompts—or layer them into prompt templates—to reduce drift, sharpen tone, and speed up workflows.

How To Use It

Skim the list, pick a phrase, and drop it into your next prompt. Each one includes a suggested line you can copy or tweak. Use them individually or combine a few to boost structure, clarity, or tone.

1. Think step-by-step

What it does: Encourages logical reasoning. The model will slow down, think in sequence, and walk through each part of the task.

Try saying: “Think this through step-by-step.”

2. Tell me what you’re going to do first

What it does: Triggers a planning mindset before the AI model generates anything. Especially ideal for multi-step tasks.

Try saying: “Explain what you’re about to do before writing anything.”

3. Act like a [person/brand]

What it does: Anchors tone and delivery to a specific communication style—great for branding.

Try saying: “Act like Apple writing for a real estate agent.”

4. Before you write, do this first

What it does: Tells the model to pause and brainstorm before jumping into the task. This improves depth and creativity.

Try saying: “Before you write the video script, list three hook options.”

5. You are a [persona/role]

What it does: Assigns the model a point of view. Helps it write like a specialist, not a generalist.

Try saying: “You are a copywriter for a luxury real estate team.”

6. Respond only with the final output

What it does: Skips the explanation and gives you clean, ready-to-use answers. Great for copy/paste workflows.

Try saying: “Don’t include any reasoning or explanation.”

7. Do you have any questions first?

What it does: Lets the model clarify instructions before beginning—especially helpful with complex prompts.

Try saying: “Ask any clarifying questions before you begin.”

Related Topics:

Prompt Like a Pro

this resource gives you a tactical list of prompt-enhancing phrases that consistently trigger better thinking, structure, or tone from ai models like chatgpt, claude, and gemini.

these aren’t just random suggestions. each phrase is backed by behavior observed in large language models. they help the model pause, plan, clarify, or write with more focus—leading to cleaner outputs with less back-and-forth.

you can insert them directly into your own prompts—or layer them into prompt templates—to reduce drift, sharpen tone, and speed up workflows.

how to use it

skim the list, pick a phrase, and drop it into your next prompt. each one includes a suggested line you can copy or tweak. use them individually or combine a few to boost structure, clarity, or tone.

1. think step-by-step

what it does: encourages logical reasoning. the model will slow down, think in sequence, and walk through each part of the task.

try saying: “think this through step-by-step.”

2. tell me what you’re going to do first

what it does: triggers a planning mindset before the ai model generates anything. especially ideal for multi-step tasks.

try saying: “explain what you’re about to do before writing anything.”

3. act like a [person/brand]

what it does: anchors tone and delivery to a specific communication style—great for branding.

try saying: “act like apple writing for a real estate agent.”

4. before you write, do this first

what it does: tells the model to pause and brainstorm before jumping into the task. this improves depth and creativity.

try saying: “before you write the video script, list three hook options.”

5. you are a [persona/role]

what it does: assigns the model a point of view. helps it write like a specialist, not a generalist.

try saying: “you are a copywriter for a luxury real estate team.”

6. respond only with the final output

what it does: skips the explanation and gives you clean, ready-to-use answers. great for copy/paste workflows.

try saying: “don’t include any reasoning or explanation.”

7. do you have any questions first?

what it does: lets the model clarify instructions before beginning—especially helpful with complex prompts.

try saying: “ask any clarifying questions before you begin.”

7 Magic Phrases That Make Prompts Better

What This Is

This resource gives you a tactical list of prompt-enhancing phrases that consistently trigger better thinking, structure, or tone from AI models like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini.

These aren’t just random suggestions. Each phrase is backed by behavior observed in large language models. They help the model pause, plan, clarify, or write with more focus—leading to cleaner outputs with less back-and-forth.

You can insert them directly into your own prompts—or layer them into prompt templates—to reduce drift, sharpen tone, and speed up workflows.

How To Use It

Skim the list, pick a phrase, and drop it into your next prompt. Each one includes a suggested line you can copy or tweak. Use them individually or combine a few to boost structure, clarity, or tone.

  1. Think step-by-step

What it does: Encourages logical reasoning. The model will slow down, think in sequence, and walk through each part of the task.

Try saying: “Think this through step-by-step.”

  1. Tell me what you’re going to do first

What it does: Triggers a planning mindset before the AI model generates anything. Especially ideal for multi-step tasks.

Try saying: “Explain what you’re about to do before writing anything.”

  1. Act like a [person/brand]

What it does: Anchors tone and delivery to a specific communication style—great for branding.

Try saying: “Act like Apple writing for a real estate agent.”

  1. Before you write, do this first

What it does: Tells the model to pause and brainstorm before jumping into the task. This improves depth and creativity.

Try saying: “Before you write the video script, list three hook options.”

  1. You are a [persona/role]

What it does: Assigns the model a point of view. Helps it write like a specialist, not a generalist.

Try saying: “You are a copywriter for a luxury real estate team.”

  1. Respond only with the final output

What it does: Skips the explanation and gives you clean, ready-to-use answers. Great for copy/paste workflows.

Try saying: “Don’t include any reasoning or explanation.”

  1. Do you have any questions first?

What it does: Lets the model clarify instructions before beginning—especially helpful with complex prompts.

Try saying: “Ask any clarifying questions before you begin.”

library.title: AI Walkthrough Workflow – System Setup
library.body:
AI Walkthrough Workflow – System Setup

What This Is

This guide walks you through producing a fully AI-generated property walkthrough video using a still listing photo. You’ll create smooth camera motion from a single image, generate a timed voiceover that matches the flow of your footage, and finish by adding a talking-head avatar overlay for a polished, agent-led listing tour.

How to Use This

Follow the steps in order. Each step builds the final video.

1. Create Walkthrough Footage (AI-Generated Camera Motion)

Gather high-quality listing photos for every room you want to feature.

If it’s not your listing, obtain required consent.

Do not materially alter the environment. AI must not add or remove architectural elements such as windows, doors, closets, railings, openings, or light sources. Always doublecheck before you publish your video(s). 

Open a generative video tool.

Primary option: Elevenlabs.io → Playground → Image & Video.

Alternatives: Artlist, Higgsfield, or Luma AI.

You need access to video models such as Kling O1, which (presently), handles motion-based instructions well.

In Elevenlabs:

Navigate to Playground → Image & Video.

In the model picker (lower left of the prompt modal), select Kling O1.

Set Aspect Ratio: 9:16.

Set Duration: 5 seconds

Set Generations: 1 to conserve tokens.

At the top, click Image Refs, then upload the reference photo for the room.

Paste the following prompt in the prompt box (modify if desired):

Generate a video walkthrough based strictly on the provided reference photo. Use a realistic filming style—such as a handheld gimbal, slow push-in, or subtle glide—while keeping all motion physically plausible and limited to what the image shows. Preserve every object, color, material, and lighting detail exactly as it appears in the reference image, and do not add, remove, or alter anything. Do not invent or imply any unseen elements, including windows, doors, railings, openings, adjacent spaces, or light sources. If something isn’t clearly visible in the photo, it must not appear in the video. The camera should move smoothly and calmly, staying fully anchored to the visual information in the reference image.

Press Generate (upper right corner).

Your clip will appear in the History tab, where you can download it.

Repeat the process for every room.

Produce one 5-second clip per image.

Download all clips to your device.

Time saver: You can have multiple generations running at the same time versus waiting for each video to finish one at a time. 

Open your video editor (CapCut, Descript, et al.).

Create a new video project

Drag clips into your timeline in the order you want.

Add transitions if you want smooth room-to-room flow.

Export the assembled walkthrough video.

2. Generate a Timed Voiceover (Claude + Elevenlabs)

Visit the Time-Synced Property Tour Voiceover Script Generator resource inside AiM: 

Paste the provided prompt into Claude.

Once you submit your prompt, it’ll ask you questions.

First, upload your exported walkthrough video.

Claude will read the footage, identify each room’s timing, and write a voiceover that matches the exact sequence and duration.

It will format the script with embedded XML cues for tone, pacing, and emphasis.

Once satisfied with the script, open Elevenlabs → Text to Speech.

Paste the script.

Choose your voice clone.

Generate the audio and download the MP3.

3. Add Your Avatar Overlay (HeyGen + CapCut)

Go to HeyGen → Avatars (left sidebar).

If you don’t have an avatar yet, create one.

If you do, hover over it and click the Create video with this avatar button.

Do not paste your script into HeyGen.

Even with an Elevenlabs voice integration connection, HeyGen doesn’t interpret XML, which causes flat delivery.

Instead:

Tap the Audio button just below where you’d type your script.

Upload the MP3 you created in Elevenlabs.

Use a 16:9 avatar style so your body doesn’t clip at the edges.

Tap Generate and download your avatar video.

Open CapCut (or your editor) again.

Drag the avatar video onto a new track above your walkthrough footage.

Select the avatar clip → navigate to Video in the project panel and find the tab labeled Remove BG → enable Auto Removal (paid feature).

Resize and position the avatar.

With your avatar clip selected in the timeline, in the player panel, click to select and move and/or resize your avatar

Place it smaller in the lower-right quadrant to keep the property front-and-center and minimize any realism inconsistencies.

Export your final video.

From the Session: This guide was introduced in AI Walkthrough Workflow.

library.title: Automated BOFU Blogger - System Setup
library.body:
Automated BOFU Blogger - System Setup

What This Is

You’re building an automated system that researches bottom-of-funnel (BOFU) topics, scores them for commercial intent, checks them against your existing content, and then writes full SEO/GEO-optimized blogs for you. Each finished draft is saved to Google Docs, ready for you to review and publish.

The system centers on one Google Sheet paired with an automated Zapier workflow. That Sheet holds your target audience, your topic categories, your compliance rules, and your full blog library. Apps Script is a built-in Google tool that lets you add small pieces of custom code to a Sheet. In this system, it quietly keeps your blog library current by pulling in your existing posts and updating the data as you publish. Zapier handles the heavy lifting — researching topics, validating intent, checking for duplicates, and generating new blogs. Google Drive stores each finished draft. You set up a few fields, review the drafts as they come in, and the automation handles the rest.

How to use this

You’re going to build this system in two parts. First, you’ll copy the master Google Sheet, fill in a few fields, and add your OpenAI API key to the Apps Script that’s already attached. Then you’ll build the Zapier workflow that researches topics, validates BOFU intent, checks against your blog library, and generates each new blog.

The process is straightforward, but it uses a few tools working together: Google Sheets, Google Docs, Google Drive, Zapier, and API keys for OpenAI and Perplexity. You don’t need to set anything up in advance. You’ll connect each tool as you go.

Once both parts are assembled, the system runs on its own. Your Sheet becomes the control center. Apps Script keeps your blog history organized and up to date. Zapier handles the full production sequence. Google Drive stores the drafts. Your job going forward is simple: review and publish the blogs it creates.

UPDATE: Before you dive into the steps, I’ve made a small adjustment to the workflow from the lecture. During the lab, several of you ran into two issues: 

exporting your existing blog posts to XML, and/or

not having an RSS feed available from your website provider.

The system works regardless, but it needed clearer guidance for those scenarios. I’ve updated the instructions (in gray) so you’ll see notes telling you when to follow or skip certain steps based on whether you have access to XML export or an RSS URL.

If you haven’t read the steps yet, this might sound abstract. Just know the guide now includes instructions that let you choose the right path without getting stuck.

– Jason(12/03/25)

Part 1: Set Up Your Sheet and Apps Script

In this part, you’ll copy the master sheet, connect your OpenAI API key, optionally load in your existing blogs, and run an Apps Script that build your blog library for the system.

Step 1: Make a copy of the master sheet

Click here to make a copy of the master sheet: Automated BOFU Blogger

When Google asks you to Make a copy, click the button.Note: Ensure you’re logged into your preferred Google account so the Sheet saves to the correct Google Drive.

Google will create your own version of the Sheet in your Drive.

All the Apps Script code you need is already attached to this Sheet. You’re just wiring it to your own account and data.

Step 2: Get your OpenAI API key

You’ll create a separate OpenAI API key (this is different from just logging into ChatGPT in your browser).

Go to https://platform.openai.com/.

Log in with the same email you use for ChatGPT.

If/when prompted, add a payment method (you can start with something small, like $5–$10 of credit).

In the left-hand sidebar, under Projects, click API keys.

Click + Create new secret key.

Copy the key OpenAI shows you.

It will only show this value once. If you copy something else before pasting, the key is lost. You’ll need to delete it and generate a new one.

Treat it like a password for your API usage.

You’ll paste this key into your Sheet’s Apps Script properties in the next step.

Step 3A: Add your API key to Apps Script

Now you’ll connect that key to the Sheet so the scripts can call OpenAI on your behalf.

In your copied Sheet—in the top menu bar—go to Extensions → Apps Script.

In the Apps Script window, click the gear icon (Project Settings) on the left.

Scroll down to Script Properties and click Add Script Property.

In the Property field, type OPENAI_API_KEY exactly.

In the Value field, paste the API key you copied from OpenAI.

Click Save.

Step 3B: Add the daily sync trigger

Heads up: Coming up in Step 5, you’ll be asked for your blog’s RSS feed URL. If you don’t have one, you can skip this step. You’ll handle ongoing blog updates a different way in Part 2 (Steps 15–16).

After your API key is saved:

In the Apps Script window, click the clock icon on the left (called Triggers).

Click + Add Trigger in the lower right corner.

Configure the trigger:

Choose which function to run: syncFromRSS

Select event source: Time-driven

Select type of time based trigger: Choose Day timer (or the interval that fits your publishing cadence)

Click Save.

Close the Apps Script tab and return to your Sheet.

What this does:

This trigger keeps your Blog Library up to date automatically so every new post you publish — even manually outside the Zap — is added to your library and factored into future topic decisions.

Step 4: (Optional) Export and prep your existing blogs

If you want the system to start with your historical blog posts, you’ll first export them from your website and convert them into clean text files.

You can skip this step if you prefer to let the library build itself over time. The Zap and RSS sync will still work. This step just gives you a full history from day one.

4A. Export your blogs to XML (if your site supports it)

Check your website or blog platform for an Export option.

Look for a way to export blog posts (often as XML).

Some platforms export all pages, not just blogs — that’s fine.

Can’t export to XML? No problem. You have two options:

Start fresh. Skip this step entirely. The system will still work—it’ll just build your library from new posts going forward.

Add blogs manually. Copy and paste the content from your existing blog posts into individual Google Docs, then save them to the Google Drive folder you will set up for your blog archive (described in item 6 of step 4B below). The system will pull them in from there.

4B. Use Claude to convert XML into text files

Note: If your website’s export tool lets you export only your blog posts (instead of every page on your site), you may need to slightly adjust the Claude prompt below so it knows it’s receiving blog-only data. The core steps remain the same — Claude will still extract each post, clean the content, and save each one as a separate text file.

Once you have the XML export:

Open a new chat in Claude.

Attach your XML export file.

Copy and paste the following prompt:

		Prompt

				Copy

You are processing an XML export from my website.
Extract every blog post.

For each matching item:
- Identify the title, link, publishing date, and content.
- Strip all HTML and preserve only readable text.
- Output each cleaned blog post as a separate file in plain text (.txt).
- Keep filenames short, based on the title (e.g., “how-to-price-your-home.txt”).

When finished, provide a zipped folder containing all text files.

			Show more

Download the zipped folder Claude gives you.

Extract the zip on your computer so you have a set of .txt blog files.

Upload all those .txt files into a single folder in Google Drive. (Make sure that folder is stored in the same Google Drive account as your Sheet.)

You’ll use that folder’s ID in the next step so Apps Script can read and import those blogs.

If your platform can’t export XML, you can skip this import and rely on your RSS feed instead. The library will then build itself from new posts going forward.

Step 5:

close the apps script tab and return to your sheet.

what this does:

this trigger keeps your blog library up to date automatically so every new post you publish — even manually outside the zap — is added to your library and factored into future topic decisions.

step 4: (optional) export and prep your existing blogs

if you want the system to start with your historical blog posts, you’ll first export them from your website and convert them into clean text files.

you can skip this step if you prefer to let the library build itself over time. the zap and rss sync will still work. this step just gives you a full history from day one.

4a. export your blogs to xml (if your site supports it)

check your website or blog platform for an export option.

look for a way to export blog posts (often as xml).

some platforms export all pages, not just blogs — that’s fine.

can’t export to xml? no problem. you have two options:

start fresh. skip this step entirely. the system will still work—it’ll just build your library from new posts going forward.

add blogs manually. copy and paste the content from your existing blog posts into individual google docs, then save them to the google drive folder you will set up for your blog archive (described in item 6 of step 4b below). the system will pull them in from there.

library.body_html:
Automated BOFU Blogger - System Setup

What This Is

You’re building an automated system that researches bottom-of-funnel (BOFU) topics, scores them for commercial intent, checks them against your existing content, and then writes full SEO/GEO-optimized blogs for you. Each finished draft is saved to Google Docs, ready for you to review and publish.

The system centers on one Google Sheet paired with an automated Zapier workflow. That Sheet holds your target audience, your topic categories, your compliance rules, and your full blog library. Apps Script is a built-in Google tool that lets you add small pieces of custom code to a Sheet. In this system, it quietly keeps your blog library current by pulling in your existing posts and updating the data as you publish. Zapier handles the heavy lifting — researching topics, validating intent, checking for duplicates, and generating new blogs. Google Drive stores each finished draft. You set up a few fields, review the drafts as they come in, and the automation handles the rest.

How to use this

You’re going to build this system in two parts. First, you’ll copy the master Google Sheet, fill in a few fields, and add your OpenAI API key to the Apps Script that’s already attached. Then you’ll build the Zapier workflow that researches topics, validates BOFU intent, checks against your blog library, and generates each new blog.

The process is straightforward, but it uses a few tools working together: Google Sheets, Google Docs, Google Drive, Zapier, and API keys for OpenAI and Perplexity. You don’t need to set anything up in advance. You’ll connect each tool as you go.

Once both parts are assembled, the system runs on its own. Your Sheet becomes the control center. Apps Script keeps your blog history organized and up to date. Zapier handles the full production sequence. Google Drive stores the drafts. Your job going forward is simple: review and publish the blogs it creates.

UPDATE: Before you dive into the steps, I’ve made a small adjustment to the workflow from the lecture. During the lab, several of you ran into two issues:

exporting your existing blog posts to XML, and/or

not having an RSS feed available from your website provider.

The system works regardless, but it needed clearer guidance for those scenarios. I’ve updated the instructions (in gray) so you’ll see notes telling you when to follow or skip certain steps based on whether you have access to XML export or an RSS URL.

If you haven’t read the steps yet, this might sound abstract. Just know the guide now includes instructions that let you choose the right path without getting stuck.

– Jason(12/03/25)

Part 1: Set Up Your Sheet and Apps Script

In this part, you’ll copy the master sheet, connect your OpenAI API key, optionally load in your existing blogs, and run an Apps Script that build your blog library for the system.

Step 1: Make a copy of the master sheet

Click here to make a copy of the master sheet: Automated BOFU Blogger

When Google asks you to Make a copy, click the button.Note: Ensure you’re logged into your preferred Google account so the Sheet saves to the correct Google Drive.

Google will create your own version of the Sheet in your Drive.

All the Apps Script code you need is already attached to this Sheet. You’re just wiring it to your own account and data.

Step 2: Get your OpenAI API key

You’ll create a separate OpenAI API key (this is different from just logging into ChatGPT in your browser).

Go to https://platform.openai.com/.

Log in with the same email you use for ChatGPT.

If/when prompted, add a payment method (you can start with something small, like $5–$10 of credit).

In the left-hand sidebar, under Projects, click API keys.

Click + Create new secret key.

Copy the key OpenAI shows you.

It will only show this value once. If you copy something else before pasting, the key is lost. You’ll need to delete it and generate a new one.

Treat it like a password for your API usage.

You’ll paste this key into your Sheet’s Apps Script properties in the next step.

Step 3A: Add your API key to Apps Script

Now you’ll connect that key to the Sheet so the scripts can call OpenAI on your behalf.

In your copied Sheet—in the top menu bar—go to Extensions → Apps Script.

In the Apps Script window, click the gear icon (Project Settings) on the left.

Scroll down to Script Properties and click Add Script Property.

In the Property field, type OPENAI_API_KEY exactly.

In the Value field, paste the API key you copied from OpenAI.

Click Save.

Step 3B: Add the daily sync trigger

Heads up: Coming up in Step 5, you’ll be asked for your blog’s RSS feed URL. If you don’t have one, you can skip this step. You’ll handle ongoing blog updates a different way in Part 2 (Steps 15–16).

After your API key is saved:

In the Apps Script window, click the clock icon on the left (called Triggers).

Click + Add Trigger in the lower right corner.

Configure the trigger:

Choose which function to run: syncFromRSS

Select event source: Time-driven

Select type of time based trigger: Choose Day timer (or the interval that fits your publishing cadence)

Click Save.

Close the Apps Script tab and return to your Sheet.

What this does:

This trigger keeps your Blog Library up to date automatically so every new post you publish — even manually outside the Zap — is added to your library and factored into future topic decisions.

Step 4: (Optional) Export and prep your existing blogs

If you want the system to start with your historical blog posts, you’ll first export them from your website and convert them into clean text files.

You can skip this step if you prefer to let the library build itself over time. The Zap and RSS sync will still work. This step just gives you a full history from day one.

4A. Export your blogs to XML (if your site supports it)

Check your website or blog platform for an Export option.

Look for a way to export blog posts (often as XML).

Some platforms export all pages, not just blogs — that’s fine.

Can’t export to XML? No problem. You have two options:

Start fresh. Skip this step entirely. The system will still work—it’ll just build your library from new posts going forward.

Add blogs manually. Copy and paste the content from your existing blog posts into individual Google Docs, then save them to the Google Drive folder you will set up for your blog archive (described in item 6 of step 4B below). The system will pull them in from there.

4B. Use Claude to convert XML into text files

Note: If your website’s export tool lets you export only your blog posts (instead of every page on your site), you may need to slightly adjust the Claude prompt below so it knows it’s receiving blog-only data. The core steps remain the same — Claude will still extract each post, clean the content, and save each one as a separate text file.

Once you have the XML export:

Open a new chat in Claude.

Attach your XML export file.

Copy and paste the following prompt:

Prompt

Copy

You are processing an XML export from my website.
Extract every blog post.

For each matching item:
- Identify the title, link, publishing date, and content.
- Strip all HTML and preserve only readable text.
- Output each cleaned blog post as a separate file in plain text (.txt).
- Keep filenames short, based on the title (e.g., “how-to-price-your-home.txt”).

When finished, provide a zipped folder containing all text files.

Show more

Download the zipped folder Claude gives you.

Extract the zip on your computer so you have a set of .txt blog files.

Upload all those .txt files into a single folder in Google Drive. (Make sure that folder is stored in the same Google Drive account as your Sheet.)

You’ll use that folder’s ID in the next step so Apps Script can read and import those blogs.

If your platform can’t export XML, you can skip this import and rely on your RSS feed instead. The library will then build itself from new posts going forward.

Step 5:

library.title: Build a Client Resource Library with NotebookLM
library.body:
Build a Client Resource Library with NotebookLM

Give Your Clients an AI Assistant Trained on Your Expertise

In February 2026, Zillow partnered with Google to launch a featured notebook in NotebookLM — a “Buying Your First Home” guide that buyers can chat with, listen to, and explore on their own. (Try it here.) If Zillow thinks this is worth building for their audience, imagine what it could do for yours — except your version isn’t generic. It’s built from your market knowledge, your process, and your expertise.

Your clients have questions. Lots of them. And most of those questions aren’t urgent — they’re the kind of thing people Google at 10 PM on a Tuesday. What if you could text them a link to an interactive, AI-powered resource — built from your expertise — that they could chat with, listen to, quiz themselves on, and explore whenever they wanted?

That’s what NotebookLM does. You feed it your sources — articles, documents, transcripts, your own notes — and it becomes an AI assistant that only knows what you told it. 

No hallucinations. No wandering off-topic. Everything it generates is grounded in the sources you gave it. And when you share a notebook with a client, they don’t just get a static document. They get the whole thing — they can ask it questions in chat, generate audio overviews to listen to on their commute, compile reports, generate explainer videos, and more. All they need is a free Google account (which most people already have).

This guide walks you through five notebooks you can build and share with clients today:

Architectural Styles & Home Elevations — so buyers know what they’re looking for before you start touring.

Pre-Listing Prep & Curb Appeal — so sellers know what to fix, paint, and stage before the photographer shows up.

What to Expect During Escrow — so first-time buyers stop calling you at 9 PM with questions.

Neighborhood Comparisons — so relocation clients can research areas on their own schedule.

Your Listing, Interactive — so buyers and showing agents arrive already engaged with your property.

Before You Start

What you’ll need:

A free Google account (NotebookLM is free at notebooklm.google.com)

Access to an AI tool with deep research or long-form output — Gemini, Perplexity, ChatGPT, Grok, or Claude all work

For Notebook #3: an AI tool with voice mode (Gemini recommended — it has a 1 million token context window, so long voice conversations won’t hit any limits)

15–20 minutes per notebook

How NotebookLM works (the 2-minute version):

Go to notebooklm.google.com and sign in with your Google account.

Click New Notebook.

Add sources — you can paste text, upload PDFs, add Google Docs, drop in website URLs, or link YouTube videos.

Once your sources are loaded, the notebook becomes an interactive AI resource. It can generate audio overviews (podcast-style—meaning, listen on the go), FAQs, study guides, mind maps, timelines, briefing docs, quizzes, slideshows, explainer videos, and more — all grounded in your sources.

Hit Share, set access to “Anyone with a link,” and send that link to your client. They open it with their Google account and get full access to chat with the notebook, generate outputs, and explore everything you built — on their own time, at their own pace.Note: They’re view-only, so they can’t change your sources or mess anything up. They can only interact with what you’ve built.

You’re not sending them a PDF. You’re giving them a personal research assistant trained on your expertise. The rest of this guide is about building great sources for five specific use cases. Each notebook below is its own section — expand the one you want to build and follow the steps.

Notebook #1: Help Buyers Identify What They Want

Architectural Styles & Home Elevations — for buyers who don’t yet have the vocabulary to describe the home they’re looking for.

You’re giving them a self-guided education on home styles — Colonial, Craftsman, Mid-Century Modern, Ranch, Tudor, Contemporary, and everything in between. They browse it, figure out what they’re drawn to, and come to your first showing with clarity instead of “I’ll know it when I see it.”

Step 1: Generate your source document.Open Gemini (gemini.google.com), Perplexity (perplexity.ai), or your preferred AI research tool. Before you paste the prompt below, make sure you select the Deep Research option in the message box — this tells the AI to do a thorough, multi-source research pass rather than a quick answer.

Fill in the placeholder first, then paste the prompt and run it:

[YOUR CITY/REGION] — Your market area. Be specific. “Nashville, Tennessee” or “Greater Orlando” works better than just a state name.

		Prompt

				Copy

Create a comprehensive guide to residential architectural styles commonly found in [YOUR CITY/REGION]. For each style, include:

- The style name and any common regional variations or nicknames
- A physical description of the exterior: roof shape, facade materials, window styles, door placement, porch or entryway features, and overall proportions
- The era when this style was most popular and any historical context
- Interior layout characteristics buyers can expect (open vs. compartmentalized floor plans, ceiling heights, typical room flow)
- Common updates or renovations homeowners make to modernize the style
- Price range context for [YOUR CITY/REGION] — where these homes typically fall relative to the broader market

Styles to cover (include all that are relevant to [YOUR CITY/REGION] and skip any that aren't common locally):
Colonial, Cape Cod, Ranch, Split-Level, Craftsman/Bungalow, Tudor, Victorian, Queen Anne, Mid-Century Modern, Contemporary/Modern, Mediterranean/Spanish Revival, French Country, Farmhouse (traditional and modern), Townhouse/Rowhome, A-Frame, Log Home, Cottage, Georgian, Federal, Prairie, Art Deco, Coastal/Beach, Southwest/Adobe, Plantation

Write this for a homebuyer audience — no jargon, no academic language. The tone should be friendly and informative, like a knowledgeable friend explaining what to look for. Use vivid descriptions that help someone picture the home from the street.

Format the guide so each style is its own clearly labeled section. End with a "How to Use This Guide" section that encourages the buyer to screenshot or bookmark the styles they're most drawn to and share them with their agent.

Include a brief contact section at the end with [YOUR NAME], [YOUR BROKERAGE], and [YOUR PHONE/EMAIL] so anyone exploring the guide knows who built it and how to reach you.

			Show more

Step 2: Review the output and add it to NotebookLM.Read through the research output before you paste it in. Does it cover the styles that are common in your market? Does anything look off or missing? Edit as needed — this is your source of truth, so make sure you’re happy with it.

Once it looks good, open your notebook in NotebookLM and click Add Source → Pasted Text. Drop the full output in and give it a clear name like “Architectural Styles Guide.”

Step 3 (Optional): Add a second source.If your MLS lets you save filtered searches by architectural style, export a PDF of sample listings for 3–4 popular styles in your market. Upload those PDFs as additional sources. This adds real, local listing examples to the notebook — but the research output alone is enough to get started.

Step 4: Share the notebook.Generate an audio overview or FAQ to seed the notebook with something your client can explore right away — then hit Share → “Anyone with a link” and text them the link. They can chat with it, generate their own study guides, listen to audio overviews, and dig deeper into any style that catches their eye.

Make it yours. This notebook gets stronger with more sources. What else would help your buyers? A PDF from a local architecture walking tour? A blog post you’ve written about popular home styles in your market? A YouTube video of you walking through different neighborhoods and pointing out the styles? Think about what you already have — or what you could create — and add it in. The notebook works with whate

  • Interior layout characteristics buyers can expect (open vs. compartmentalized floor plans, ceiling heights, typical room flow)
  • Common updates or renovations homeowners make to modernize the style
  • Price range context for [YOUR CITY/REGION] — where these homes typically fall relative to the broader market

Styles to cover (include all that are relevant to [YOUR CITY/REGION] and skip any that aren't common locally):
Colonial, Cape Cod, Ranch, Split-Level, Craftsman/Bungalow, Tudor, Victorian, Queen Anne, Mid-Century Modern, Contemporary/Modern, Mediterranean/Spanish Revival, French Country, Farmhouse (traditional and modern), Townhouse/Rowhome, A-Frame, Log Home, Cottage, Georgian, Federal, Prairie, Art Deco, Coastal/Beach, Southwest/Adobe, Plantation

Write this for a homebuyer audience — no jargon, no academic language. The tone should be friendly and informative, like a knowledgeable friend explaining what to look for. Use vivid descriptions that help someone picture the home from the street.

Format the guide so each style is its own clearly labeled section. End with a "How to Use This Guide" section that encourages the buyer to screenshot or bookmark the styles they're most drawn to and share them with their agent.

Include a brief contact section at the end with [YOUR NAME], [YOUR BROKERAGE], and [YOUR PHONE/EMAIL] so anyone exploring the guide knows who built it and how to reach you.

Show more

Step 2: Review the output and add it to NotebookLM.Read through the research output before you paste it in. Does it cover the styles that are common in your market? Does anything look off or missing? Edit as needed — this is your source of truth, so make sure you’re happy with it.

Once it looks good, open your notebook in NotebookLM and click Add Source → Pasted Text. Drop the full output in and give it a clear name like “Architectural Styles Guide.”

Step 3 (Optional): Add a second source.If your MLS lets you save filtered searches by architectural style, export a PDF of sample listings for 3–4 popular styles in your market. Upload those PDFs as additional sources. This adds real, local listing examples to the notebook — but the research output alone is enough to get started.

Step 4: Share the notebook.Generate an audio overview or FAQ to seed the notebook with something your client can explore right away — then hit Share → “Anyone with a link” and text them the link. They can chat with it, generate their own study guides, listen to audio overviews, and dig deeper into any style that catches their eye.

Make it yours. This notebook gets stronger with more sources. What else would help your buyers? A PDF from a local architecture walking tour? A blog post you’ve written about popular home styles in your market? A YouTube video of you walking through different neighborhoods and pointing out the styles? Think about what you already have — or what you could create — and add it in. The notebook works with whate
library.description: Turn 4 ready-made prompts into shareable NotebookLM notebooks your clients can chat with, listen to, and explore on their own time.

library.title: Claude Cowork: YouTube to Blog Agent
library.body:
Claude Cowork: YouTube to Blog Agent

What This Is

You’re about to set up an AI agent that turns your YouTube videos into blog posts — automatically, every day, while you’re doing something else entirely.

By the end of this guide, you’ll have a system that checks your channel for new videos, writes SEO-optimized blog posts from the transcripts, saves them to your computer, and messages you when they’re ready to review. You describe it once. It runs every day.

You’ll need four things: the Claude Desktop app (Mac or Windows, any paid plan), the Claude in Chrome extension, a YouTube channel, and a blog.

You’re already sharing videos. That content has real value. But right now, the only people finding it are the ones watching on YouTube. A blog post takes that same video and puts it somewhere else (that Google indexes), AI models cite, and potential clients discover when they search for exactly what you’re talking about. Same content. More reach. More visibility. More chances to be the agent someone finds at the right moment.

The problem? Nobody has time to transcribe a video, write an optimized post, add schema markup, find internal links, and draft it into their blog. Every single day or week. That’s the kind of repetitive, high-value work AI agents were built for. This workflow handles it. Every day. Without you lifting a finger.

Before we get into the build, it’s worth understanding what makes this different from the AI you’re probably used to.

Chat AI answers your questions. Agentic AI does your work.

Here’s how most people use AI today:

Type a prompt

Get text back

Copy it, paste it somewhere else

Reformat it

Realize it doesn’t match your brand

Fix it by hand

That’s not AI doing your work — that’s AI adding to it.

Agentic AI flips this completely. You describe the outcome. The AI makes a plan, connects to your apps, navigates websites, produces finished documents, and delivers. The work is done.

So what is Claude Cowork? It’s a feature in the Claude Desktop app where you describe a task, step away, and come back to finished work. It reads your files, browses the web, creates documents, and delivers results — on demand or on a daily schedule. It’s Claude that does things, not just says things.

It works in your local files. Real documents — spreadsheets, slide decks, reports, HTML files — saved to a folder on your computer. Not text in a chat window. Actual files.

It runs ona schedule. Set a workflow to fire daily, weekly, or on demand. No manual trigger needed. You set it and walk away.

It uses skills and connectors. Skills teach it how to do things (like writing a blog post in your voice, with your SEO preferences). Connectors let it talk to the outside world — your browser, your messages, your email.

What you’re building today — a Claude Cowork automation that:

Scans your YouTube channel every day for new videos — long-form and Shorts

Pulls the transcript from each new video

Writes a complete blog post — SEO title, meta description, AI summary, embedded video, internal links, schema markup, CTAs

Saves it to your computer as a ready-to-publish file (or drafts it directly to your blog — your call)

Notifies you when it’s done — via iMessage, Slack, or your preferred channel

How to Use It

The setup takes about 15-30 minutes. Six steps.

Step 1: Run the Intake Prompt

This is a conversation you have with Claude in a regular chat — not Cowork. Think of it as the onboarding interview. Claude asks you a handful of questions about your channel, your blog, your audience, and how you want things delivered. Then it generates everything you need — personalized skills and a ready-to-paste Cowork prompt — as downloadable files.

First, attach both skill template files to the chat before pasting the prompt. Tap below to download a zip file with both skill templates, then copy the prompt beneath it.

yt-video-blog-writer-template.md — The blog writing skill template. Claude personalizes this with your name, bio, audience, and CTA during the conversation.

youtube-scraper-template.md — The YouTube scraper skill. Works universally — no personalization needed.

YouTube-to-Blog-Skill-TemplatesDownload

Answer the questions (there are about 7 or 8), then download all the files Claude generates for you. You’ll get your personalized skills and a Cowork prompt — ready to use.

Here’s the intake prompt — copy and paste it into Claude:

		Prompt

				Copy

You are a setup assistant that helps users create a personalized Claude Cowork scheduled task. This task will automatically turn their YouTube videos into SEO-optimized blog posts.

**Two template files should be attached to this conversation:** `yt-video-blog-writer-template.md` and `youtube-scraper-template.md`. These are your reference templates. You will personalize the yt-video-blog-writer template based on the user's answers. The youtube-scraper template is already universal and doesn't need personalization — deliver it as-is.

Your job is to:
1. Interview the user to collect their details
2. Personalize the yt-video-blog-writer skill from the attached template
3. Deliver the youtube-scraper skill as-is
4. Generate a complete Cowork prompt as a downloadable .txt file
5. Walk them through setup step by step

If the template files are not attached, tell the user: "I need two files attached to this conversation to get started: `yt-video-blog-writer-template.md` and `youtube-scraper-template.md`. These are the skill templates I'll personalize for you. Please attach them and we'll begin."

## How This Conversation Works

Ask the questions below ONE AT A TIME. Wait for the user's answer before moving to the next question. Be conversational and brief — don't over-explain. If something needs clarification, keep it to one sentence.

## Questions to Ask

### Question 1: YouTube Channel
"What's your YouTube channel URL? (Example: https://www.youtube.com/yourchannel)"

### Question 2: Blog URL
"What's your blog or website URL? (Example: https://yoursite.com)"

### Question 3: Blog Publishing
"Once the blog post is written, how do you want it delivered? Pick one:

A) **HTML file on my computer** — You open the file, copy the content, and paste it into your blog yourself. Works with any blog platform. Easiest and safest option.

B) **Claude navigates to my blog and posts it** — The automation uses Claude's Chrome extension to open your blog's admin area, create a new post, and save a draft. On the first run, Claude will learn your blog's interface and build a custom publishing routine. A few things to know:
   - Browser automation can break when your blog platform updates its interface
   - Anthropic warns against using Claude in Chrome for sensitive information
   - This is the most fragile part of the workflow and may occasionally need manual intervention
   - Your blog login credentials will pass through the browser (not through the AI chat)

Option A is the safer, simpler choice. You still get the fully written blog post — you just paste it in yourself."

If they pick B, ask: "What's the URL to your blog's admin or dashboard? (Example: https://yoursite.com/wp-admin or https://yoursite.squarespace.com)"

### Question 4: Blog Author
"What name should appear as the author on these blog posts? And give me a 2-3 sentence bio — who you are, what you do, who you serve. This goes at the bottom of every post for SEO credibility."

### Question 5: Target Audience
"Who reads your blog? Describe your ideal reader in one sentence. (Example: 'Small business owners learning to market with AI')"

### Question 6: Call to Action
"Is there a product, service, membership, or offer you want promoted in the blog posts? If yes, give me the name, a one-line description, and the URL. If not, just say 'no CTA.'"

### Question 7: Notification
"How do you want to be notified when a blog draft is ready?

A) **iMessage** — Text to your iPhone. Mac only.
B) **Slack** — Message to a Slack channel or DM.
C) **Zapier** — Triggers a Zapier action that can send an SMS, email, push notification, or anything else. Most flexible.
D) **No notifi

library.title: Claude Skills Agent Starter Pack
library.body:
Claude Skills Agent Starter Pack

What This Is

Nine ready-to-use Claude Skills — plus one setup prompt that personalizes all of them to your brand, market, and voice in a single conversation. You paste the prompt, answer a few questions, and Claude builds you a custom toolkit that activates automatically every time you work. No copying and pasting instructions over and over. No re-explaining who you are. You set it up once and it just works.

How to Use It

Every time you open a new chat and start from scratch, Claude doesn’t know your market, your audience, your voice, or your standards. So the output sounds generic. You fix it, re-explain, try again — and that cycle eats time you don’t have.

Skills eliminate the cycle. They’re instruction files that tell Claude how to do specific tasks your way. Write a listing description? Claude knows your market, your noun-dense style, your compliance guardrails. Draft a newsletter? Claude knows your ESP, your section structure, your tone. The Skill fires automatically when it recognizes the task. You don’t invoke it. You don’t paste anything. You just ask for what you need and the quality is already there.

This pack gives you nine of them — covering listings, content, marketing, and data analysis — built specifically for real estate agents.

What you’ll need:

Any Claude subscription level—free or paid. 

The 9 Skill files (download the zip below)

About 10–15 minutes to answer the setup questions

Step by step:

Download the zip file and extract it. You’ll see 10 .md files — the setup prompt and 9 Skills.

Open a new Claude chat.

Open the setup-prompt.md file in any text editor (TextEdit, Notepad, VS Code — whatever you have). Copy the entire contents.

Paste it into the Claude chat as your first message.

Attach all 9 Skill files to that same message using the attachment button (the paperclip or + icon). Attach them all at once — don’t send them one at a time.

Send the message. Claude will read the setup prompt and begin asking you questions about your brand, market, and voice. Answer them honestly and specifically — the more detail you give here, the better your Skills perform later.

Claude returns 9 personalized Skills. Each one appears as a formatted .md file with a “Copy to Skills” button. Click that button on each one to save it to your Skills.

You’re done. The next time you ask Claude to write a listing description, draft a newsletter, or analyze MLS data, the relevant Skill fires automatically. You don’t need to mention it or invoke it — Claude recognizes the task and applies the right Skill on its own.

Claude-Skills-Agent-Starter-PackDownload

		Prompt

				Copy

---
name: Real Estate Skills Setup
description: Personalizes a pack of 9 real estate marketing Skills based on the users brand, market, and preferences. Use when uploading the starter pack.
---

# Real Estate Skills Starter Pack — Setup Prompt

You are helping a real estate agent personalize a pack of 9 Claude Skills for their business. The user has attached the generic Skill files. Your job is to ask the right questions, then rewrite each Skill with their specific details filled in.

## Intake Process

Ask the following questions in order. Group them into 2–3 messages so you're not overwhelming the user. Wait for answers before proceeding.

### Brand Identity
1. What is your full name (as it should appear in all marketing)?
2. What is your brokerage name?
3. What is your title or role? (e.g., REALTOR®, Listing Agent, Broker Associate)
4. What is your market area? (City, state, neighborhoods, ZIP codes — be specific)
5. Do you have brand colors? If so, share the hex codes. If not, describe the vibe you're going for (modern, warm, luxury, approachable, etc.).
6. Do you have brand fonts? If so, name them. If not, that's fine — skip this.
7. Describe your tone of voice in 3–5 words. (Examples: "confident, conversational, sharp" or "warm, friendly, approachable" or "luxury, polished, authoritative")
8. Are there any words, phrases, or topics you always want to include — or always want to avoid? (Examples: always mention a specific neighborhood, never use the word "nestled," always sign off with a specific tagline)
9. What is your standard sign-off for emails and written communications? (e.g., "Best, [Name]" or "Talk soon, [Name]" or your full signature block)

### Business Context
10. Who is your primary audience? (e.g., sellers, buyers, investors, relocations, luxury, first-time buyers — pick your dominant focus)
11. What is your primary call-to-action? (e.g., "Book a free home value review," "DM me," "Call or text me at [number]," "Visit [website]")
12. What email service provider (ESP) do you use? (Mailchimp, Constant Contact, Follow Up Boss, Flodesk, Klaviyo, etc.)

### Your Market (for the MLS Data Analyzer and Price Reduction Skills)
13. What do buyers in your market pay a premium for? (Examples: updated kitchens, big lots, walkability, acreage, views, parking, new construction, being in a specific neighborhood — whatever drives value where you work)
14. How do you pull comps? (Same subdivision only? Same ZIP? Within a certain radius? By price bracket? Describe how you decide what counts as a comparable property.)
15. What kills a deal in your market? (Examples: no garage, flood zone, busy road, septic, HOA over a certain amount, outdated kitchen — the features that make buyers walk away)
16. When you sit down with a seller, what numbers do you show them first? (Which 2–3 data points do you always lead with when advising on price?)

### Content Style
17. Upload 2–3 examples of your best writing — past emails, social captions, listing descriptions, anything that sounds like you. This is optional but dramatically improves the voice matching across all 9 Skills.

## What To Do With the Answers

Once you have all answers:

1. Read each attached .md Skill file.
2. Rewrite each one, replacing all placeholder text (marked with [BRACKETS] or generic language) with the user's specific details.
3. Integrate the user's tone, vocabulary preferences, market area, and audience throughout.
4. If the user provided writing samples, analyze their voice patterns (sentence length, phrasing habits, vocabulary choices, rhythm) and weave those patterns into the voice-related instructions in each Skill.
5. Output each personalized Skill as a complete, properly formatted .md file — with YAML frontmatter intact — that the user can copy directly to their Skills.
6. Present them one at a time in the order listed below. After each one, briefly explain what it does in 1–2 sentences.

## Skill Order

1. Brand Style Guide
2. Listing Remarks Writer
3. Listing Photo Captioner
4. MLS Data Analyzer
5. Price Reduction Angle Generator
6. Instagram Caption Writer
7. Reel Script Writer
8. Email Newsletter Writer
9. Blog Post Writer

## Important Rules

- Do NOT fabricate any details the user didn't provide. If information is missing, ask for it.
- Do NOT include any references to specific brands, academies, or training programs.
- Each Skill must remain singularly focused on one task. Do not merge or combine Skills.
- Keep all YAML frontmatter (name and description) intact and properly formatted.
- The Brand Style Guide Skill should be completed first because every subsequent Skill can reference it.

			Show more

The 9 Skills in the pack:

1. Brand Style Guide — The foundation. Stores your name, brokerage, colors, fonts, tone of voice, standard sign-off, and audience. Every other Skill references this one, so your brand stays consistent across everything Claude produces.

2. Listing Remarks Writer — Writes MLS listing descriptions optimized for AI-powered home search — ChatGPT with Zillow, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews. Uses noun-dense, keyword-rich language so your listings surface when buyers search conversationally. If it’s not in the description, AI can’t find it.

3. Listing Photo Captioner — Upload your listing photos and Claude identifies each room, spots the key features, and writes MLS-ready captions. Accurate, compliant, and searchable — one photo at a time or in batches of up to 10.

library.body_html:
Claude Skills Agent Starter Pack

What This Is

Nine ready-to-use Claude Skills — plus one setup prompt that personalizes all of them to your brand, market, and voice in a single conversation. You paste the prompt, answer a few questions, and Claude builds you a custom toolkit that activates automatically every time you work. No copying and pasting instructions over and over. No re-explaining who you are. You set it up once and it just works.

How to Use It

Every time you open a new chat and start from scratch, Claude doesn’t know your market, your audience, your voice, or your standards. So the output sounds generic. You fix it, re-explain, try again — and that cycle eats time you don’t have.

Skills eliminate the cycle. They’re instruction files that tell Claude how to do specific tasks your way. Write a listing description? Claude knows your market, your noun-dense style, your compliance guardrails. Draft a newsletter? Claude knows your ESP, your section structure, your tone. The Skill fires automatically when it recognizes the task. You don’t invoke it. You don’t paste anything. You just ask for what you need and the quality is already there.

This pack gives you nine of them — covering listings, content, marketing, and data analysis — built specifically for real estate agents.

What you’ll need:

Any Claude subscription level—free or paid.

The 9 Skill files (download the zip below)

About 10–15 minutes to answer the setup questions

Step by step:

Download the zip file and extract it. You’ll see 10 .md files — the setup prompt and 9 Skills.

Open a new Claude chat.

Open the setup-prompt.md file in any text editor (TextEdit, Notepad, VS Code — whatever you have). Copy the entire contents.

Paste it into the Claude chat as your first message.

Attach all 9 Skill files to that same message using the attachment button (the paperclip or + icon). Attach them all at once — don’t send them one at a time.

Send the message. Claude will read the setup prompt and begin asking you questions about your brand, market, and voice. Answer them honestly and specifically — the more detail you give here, the better your Skills perform later.

Claude returns 9 personalized Skills. Each one appears as a formatted .md file with a “Copy to Skills” button. Click that button on each one to save it to your Skills.

You’re done. The next time you ask Claude to write a listing description, draft a newsletter, or analyze MLS data, the relevant Skill fires automatically. You don’t need to mention it or invoke it — Claude recognizes the task and applies the right Skill on its own.

library.title: Glossary of AiM and AI Terms
library.body:
Glossary of AiM and AI Terms

What This IsEvery tool, session, and resource inside Ai Marketing Academy (AiM) comes with its own vocabulary—and the broader world of artificial intelligence (AI) adds a lot more. This glossary covers the most important terms you’ll run into so the jargon doesn’t slow you down. It’s not meant to be read cover to cover. Bookmark it, come back when something comes up, and keep moving.

Terms Defined:

Generative AI (GenAI): AI that creates new content like text, images, or audio by analyzing patterns in its training data.

Model: The computational structure in AI that generates outputs, such as OpenAI’s DALL·E for images.

Training: The process of teaching AI patterns and relationships using large datasets, like books or images.

Inference: The process where AI uses its training to produce outputs or predictions from given input.

Prompt: A user-provided input or instruction that guides AI in generating a specific response.

Token: A segment of text, such as a word or subword, that AI processes during training or content generation.

Context Window: The range of text or data an AI model considers when generating a response or output. Larger context windows allow AI to reference more information simultaneously.

Grounded: Ensures AI responses are based on factual, accurate, or real-world information rather than solely predictive patterns.

LLM (Large Language Model): A specialized AI trained on extensive text data to generate and interpret language.

Multimodal: AI capable of understanding and generating multiple types of data, such as text, images, and audio.

Fine-Tune: Customizing a pre-trained AI model with specific data to enhance its performance for certain tasks.

Reasoning: AI models or methods designed to mimic logical thought processes, allowing the AI to make decisions based on structured analysis.

Non-Reasoning: AI models that primarily generate responses based on pattern recognition without explicit logical or analytical processes.

Hybrid: AI approaches or models that combine elements of both reasoning and non-reasoning methodologies to optimize performance.

Hallucinations: Instances when AI generates outputs that appear plausible but are factually incorrect or nonsensical.

API (Application Programming Interface): A set of protocols allowing software components or systems to communicate, crucial for integrating AI models into applications.

Deep Research: Multi-step research that autonomously searches the web, analyzes sources, and synthesizes a well-cited report.

Agentic: Describes AI systems capable of acting independently, performing complex tasks, and making decisions with minimal human oversight.

library.title: How to Create a ChatGPT Custom GPT
library.body:
How to Create a ChatGPT Custom GPT

How to Create a Custom GPT

Log in to ChatGPT

Access the platform via the web (not the app).

Tap Your Profile Icon

Click on your profile image in the top right corner of the screen.

Go to “My GPTs”

From the dropdown menu, click “My GPTs.”

Create a New GPT

Click “Create a GPT.” You’ll be given two options: Create or Configure.

Create: This lets you chat with the GPT like a normal conversation, and it will summarize the key takeaways to form instructions. Note that this method may miss details.

Configure: This is the recommended approach for better control. It allows you to manually set all instructions and behaviors.

Fill in the Fields

Name & Description: Provide a unique name and a concise description of your GPT.

Icon/Favicon: Upload an image or use DALL-E to create one.

Knowledge Upload: You can upload documents, spreadsheets, or other files to provide your GPT with context or information.

Pro Tips: Clearly instruct your GPT on how to use this uploaded knowledge in its responses.

Instructions Section: Use the guidance from your lectures and labs to craft detailed instructions for your GPT’s behavior and how it should handle different types of queries.

Conversation Starters: Set up prompts that users can use to start a conversation. Reference these in your instructions to tailor the GPT’s responses effectively.

Tap “Create” & Save Your GPT

Once you’re finished, tap “Create” in the top right corner. You can then choose whether to save your custom GPT privately or make it publicly available.

From the Session: This guide was introduced in Personalize Your ChatGPT.

library.body_html:
How to Create a ChatGPT Custom GPT

How to Create a Custom GPT

Log in to ChatGPT

Access the platform via the web (not the app).

Tap Your Profile Icon

Click on your profile image in the top right corner of the screen.

Go to “My GPTs”

From the dropdown menu, click “My GPTs.”

Create a New GPT

Click “Create a GPT.” You’ll be given two options: Create or Configure.

Create: This lets you chat with the GPT like a normal conversation, and it will summarize the key takeaways to form instructions. Note that this method may miss details.

Configure: This is the recommended approach for better control. It allows you to manually set all instructions and behaviors.

Fill in the Fields

Name & Description: Provide a unique name and a concise description of your GPT.

Icon/Favicon: Upload an image or use DALL-E to create one.

Knowledge Upload: You can upload documents, spreadsheets, or other files to provide your GPT with context or information.

Pro Tips: Clearly instruct your GPT on how to use this uploaded knowledge in its responses.

Instructions Section: Use the guidance from your lectures and labs to craft detailed instructions for your GPT’s behavior and how it should handle different types of queries.

Conversation Starters: Set up prompts that users can use to start a conversation. Reference these in your instructions to tailor the GPT’s responses effectively.

Tap “Create” & Save Your GPT

Once you’re finished, tap “Create” in the top right corner. You can then choose whether to save your custom GPT privately or make it publicly available.

From the Session: This guide was introduced in Personalize Your ChatGPT.
library.description: Learn to create a Custom GPT in ChatGPT to tailor responses and instruct it for specific tasks.

library.title: How to Personalize Your ChatGPT Account
library.body:
How to Personalize Your ChatGPT Account

What This Is

This resource contains a working example of how to use ChatGPT’s Personalization feature—available to Plus, Team, and Pro users—to shape how ChatGPT responds to you. These settings help tailor the assistant’s tone, priorities, and awareness of your work so that it behaves more like a reliable partner and less like a generic AI.

You’ll find sample answers to each personalization section, including a custom description of your work, a clear list of preferred traits in ChatGPT’s behavior, and a note about your broader goals and expectations. This exact setup helps maintain consistency and clarity across sessions, especially when using ChatGPT for professional or high-context work like content creation, marketing, or training development.

How To Use It

Log into your ChatGPT Plus, Team, or Pro account

Tap your profile image in the top-right corner (on the website—location may vary in the apps).

Select Settings

From the left-hand menu, choose Personalization, then select Custom Instructions.

You’ll see four open-ended fields:

What should ChatGPT call you?

What do you do?

What traits should ChatGPT have?

Anything else ChatGPT should know about you?

You’ll also see a section called Advanced—tap to expand it, and check all the boxes to enable additional tools and features like Web Search, Canvas, and Advanced Voice.

Use the examples provided below as templates or inspiration. Customize the wording to reflect your own goals, tone, and workflow needs.

This setup acts as a baseline that ChatGPT will automatically refer to in your chats—without needing to restate instructions each time. It’s especially helpful for professionals who rely on ChatGPT to think with them, not just for them.

Question #1: What should ChatGPT call you?

		Prompt

				Copy

Jason

			Show more

👉 Just to clarify, you’ll enter your own details—these are simply my examples to use as a reference or starting point.

Question #2: What do you do?

		Prompt

				Copy

I'm a speaker, business coach, and content creator who trains real estate professionals in Ai and marketing. I lead a training membership, Ai Marketing Academy (also known as "AiM"), where I develop educational content and support members’ growth. Separately, I offer business coaching through Tom Ferry, the #1 coach in real estate. I use ChatGPT for business strategy, content creation, training development, and personal support across a wide range of needs.

			Show more

Question #3: What traits should ChatGPT have?

		Prompt

				Copy

1. Always provide accurate information. • Never hallucinate or exaggerate; explicitly state uncertainties.2. No insincere praise or pandering. • Only acknowledge genuine excellence or insight.3. Keep answers concise, clear, and structured. • Eliminate unnecessary elaboration or jargon.4. Write in a conversational, semi-formal tone.5. Sound natural and human. • Avoid robotic, formulaic language6. Leverage prior context intelligently. • Don’t repeat known information unless directly relevant.7. Offer practical, actionable advice. • Prioritize real-world utility (especially marketing, AI, real estate).8. Be iterative and collaborative. • Invite adjustments and refinements.9. Maintain proper punctuation and style. • Use Oxford commas and natural contractions. • Avoid starting sentences with “Let’s.” • Omit “Unlock” in marketing contexts.10. Follow these universally unless explicitly instructed otherwise.

			Show more

Question #4: Anything else ChatGPT should know about you?

		Prompt

				Copy

I'm a multifaceted user who relies on ChatGPT for a wide range of purposes—professional tasks, content creation, client support, training development, and personal inquiries. I value accurate, practical, and clear responses that adapt to context. I want ChatGPT to remain flexible and not assume a narrow use case, since my needs can vary from strategic business planning to casual curiosity.

			Show more

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

From the Session: This guide was introduced in Personalize Your ChatGPT.

library.title: How to Set Up a Meta Business Manager
library.body:
How to Set Up a Meta Business Manager

What this is

Meta Business Manager is the “command center” for everything you run on the ads side of Facebook and Instagram.

It gives you one secure place to organize and control your Pages, ad accounts, audiences, and other marketing tools—so you can run campaigns, share access, and connect integrations, all from one central hub.

Inside Business Manager:

Inside Business Manager, you store the business assets your ad campaigns depend on:

Facebook Pages and Instagram accounts – the public profiles your audience sees, grouped here so you can grant or revoke access without sharing personal logins. 

NOTE: Only Facebook Pages—not personal profiles—can be added to Business Manager, and only Instagram Professional accounts (Business or Creator) are eligible. Meta only allows ads from these professional accounts, which is why they’re the only ones supported here.

Ad accounts – the workspaces inside Ads Manager where your campaigns are created, managed, and billed.

Pixels and other tracking tools – code snippets that track actions (like website visits or button clicks) and feed results back into Ads Manager.

Custom Audiences – audiences built from your own data, like people who visited your site, watched your videos, or appear on a customer list you upload (e.g., from a CRM or webinar signup).

NOTE: To sync contacts automatically from tools like Zapier or Make, your Custom Audience must live inside a Business-Manager-owned ad account.

Business Manager Benefits:

Secure access sharing – Grant permission to teammates, VAs, or ad agencies without giving them your Facebook or Instagram Page logins.

Access to advanced tools and integrations – Use tools like Zapier, Make, and the Meta API to sync audiences, track events, and automate workflows—features only available through a Business-Manager-owned ad account.

Support for multiple brands or entities – Manage several Pages, ad accounts, or Instagram profiles from a single dashboard—perfect for teams, brokerages, or anyone with more than one brand presence.

Key TermSimple ExplanationPersonal profileYour individual Facebook login—e.g., Jane Doe. Required to create and access Business Manager.Facebook PageA public-facing business profile (e.g., “Acme Realty”) that you use for posts and ads.Instagram Professional AccountA Business or Creator Instagram profile—required for running ads and connecting to Business Manager.Personal Ad AccountThe default ad account created when you boost posts from your Facebook Page or Instagram account. It’s owned by the Page but not connected to Business Manager, which means it won’t support Custom Audiences, Zapier, or other advanced tools.Business Ad AccountAn ad account created or owned inside Business Manager. Required for uploading customer lists, using third-party integrations like Zapier or Make, and accessing Meta’s full ad platform features.Business ManagerThe backend hub where you control your ad accounts, Pages, Pixels, permissions, and integrations—required for advanced advertising and automation.Business SuiteThe dashboard for organic posting, scheduling, replying to DMs, and viewing basic performance insights across Facebook and Instagram.

How to use this

Before you start: Open a laptop or desktop browser (not your phone app) and go to business.facebook.com/overview — not just business.facebook.com. Otherwise, Meta may redirect you into Business Suite (their organic content dashboard), which doesn’t give you access to the full Business Manager setup.

Create your Business Manager

Log in with your personal Facebook profile (the one you normally use).

Note: Business Manager isn’t created from a personal account, but you must be logged into your personal Facebook profile to access it. That profile serves as your identity, but all assets inside Business Manager remain separate and business-owned.

Visit business.facebook.com/overview, click Create account, and follow the prompts.

Confirm your business details

Enter your business name, your name, work email, phone, and address.

Click Confirm. Check your inbox and click the link in Meta’s email to finish verification.

Open Business Settings

Inside the left-hand menu, click ⚙︎ Settings → Business Settings.

You’re now in the “back office” where you add assets.

Confirm your own admin role

In Business Settings → Users → People, find your name and confirm you’re listed as an Admin (not just Employee).

You’ll be able to assign yourself access to Pages, ad accounts, and other assets once they’re added in the next steps.

Why it matters: Creating the Business Manager makes you the owner, but it doesn’t automatically assign you access to every asset. You’ll need to add that access manually once assets are inside your Business Manager.

Add or claim your Facebook Page

In the left menu choose Accounts → Pages → Add.

Pick one:

Create – make a brand-new Page.

Claim – pull in a Page you already admin.

Request access – ask a client to grant you access.

Create or claim an Ad Account

Go to Accounts → Ad Accounts → Add.

Choose Create (new) or Claim (existing ID).

Meta lets each personal profile create up to two new ad accounts.

Add a payment method

In Billing → Payment methods click Add and enter a credit card, PayPal, or direct debit.

Ads and Custom Audiences will not run without this step.

Connect your Instagram Business or Creator profile

Choose Accounts → Instagram accounts → Add.

Log in and approve the connection.

(Optional) Add extra assets now

Data Sources → Pixels – track website visitors.

Custom Conversions, Catalogs, WhatsApp numbers – add these if you plan to use them later.

Invite teammates or partners

Click People or Partners, depending on who you’re adding. For individuals, go to People → Add, enter their work email, and choose Employee or Admin. For outside businesses or agencies, go to Partners → Add and enter their Business Manager ID.

Note: Even if you invite someone using their work email, they’ll still need to log in with their personal Facebook profile to accept the invite and access Business Manager. The email you enter doesn’t have to match their Facebook login—it’s just used for the invitation.

Flip on only the assets they need (Page, ad account, pixel, etc.).

Secure and certify your account

In Business Settings → Security Center turn on two-factor authentication.

Accept any required Meta terms—such as Custom Audience Terms, Lead Ads Terms, or Housing Category certifications—depending on which tools you’ll be using.

Choose your Primary Page under Business Info. This links your Business Manager identity to one of your Facebook Pages. It’s often required for verification and public transparency in ads.

Once these steps are done, you’ll have a properly-wired Business Manager that can receive contacts from Zapier into a Custom Audience—exactly what you need for your upcoming automation.

DISCLAIMER: This resource provides prompts, instructions, and content to help professionals use AI tools more effectively. Because AI-generated outputs can vary, it’s your responsibility to review and refine them for accuracy, relevance, and alignment with applicable laws, industry standards, and your specific business objectives.

From the Session: This guide was introduced in Automate with AI.

Confirm your business details
